* [Fall of the Western Roman Empire](/wiki/Fall_of_the_Western_Roman_Empire "Fall of the Western Roman Empire") * [Spread of Islam](/wiki/Spread_of_Islam "Spread of Islam") * [Treaty of Verdun](/wiki/Treaty_of_Verdun "Treaty of Verdun") * [East–West Schism](/wiki/East%E2%80%93West_Schism "East–West Schism") * [Crusades](/wiki/Crusades "Crusades") * [Magna Carta](/wiki/Magna_Carta "Magna Carta") * [Hundred Years' War](/wiki/Hundred_Years%27_War "Hundred Years' War") * [Black Death](/wiki/Black_Death "Black Death") * [Fall of Constantinople](/wiki/Fall_of_Constantinople "Fall of Constantinople") * [Exploration of North America](/wiki/Exploration_of_North_America "Exploration of North America")

or **medieval period** lasted approximately from the 5th to late 15th centuries, comparable with the [post-classical](/wiki/Post-classical "Post-classical") period of [global history](/wiki/World_history_(field) "World history (field)"). The medieval period is the middle epoch of the three traditional divisions of Western history: [classical antiquity](/wiki/Classical_antiquity "Classical antiquity"), the medieval period, and the [modern period](/wiki/Modern_period "Modern period"). The medieval period is itself subdivided into the [Early](/wiki/Early_Middle_Ages "Early Middle Ages"), [High](/wiki/High_Middle_Ages "High Middle Ages"), and [Late Middle Ages](/wiki/Late_Middle_Ages "Late Middle Ages").

European history from the 5th to 15th centuries

This article is about medieval Europe. For a global history of the period between the 5th and 15th centuries, see [Post-classical history](/wiki/Post-classical_history "Post-classical history"). For other uses, see [Middle Ages (disambiguation)](/wiki/Middle_Ages_(disambiguation) "Middle Ages (disambiguation)").

In the [history of Europe](/wiki/History_of_Europe "History of Europe"), the **Middle Ages** or **medieval period** lasted approximately from the 5th to late 15th centuries, comparable with the [post-classical](/wiki/Post-classical "Post-classical") period of [global history](/wiki/World_history_(field) "World history (field)"). The medieval period is the middle epoch of the three traditional divisions of Western history: [classical antiquity](/wiki/Classical_antiquity "Classical antiquity"), the medieval period, and the [modern period](/wiki/Modern_period "Modern period"). The medieval period is itself subdivided into the [Early](/wiki/Early_Middle_Ages "Early Middle Ages"), [High](/wiki/High_Middle_Ages "High Middle Ages"), and [Late Middle Ages](/wiki/Late_Middle_Ages "Late Middle Ages").

The Middle Ages began with the [fall of the Western Roman Empire](/wiki/Fall_of_the_Western_Roman_Empire "Fall of the Western Roman Empire") and transitioned into the [Modern age](/wiki/Modern_era "Modern era") during the [Renaissance](/wiki/Renaissance "Renaissance"), the [Reformation](/wiki/Reformation "Reformation"), the European [Wars of Religion](/wiki/European_wars_of_religion "European wars of religion") and the [Age of Discovery](/wiki/Age_of_Discovery "Age of Discovery"). [Early Modernity](/wiki/Early_modern_period "Early modern period") is a single term adopted to describe this end-stage of the period, during which medieval Europes shades into the modern period.

[Population decline](/wiki/Population_decline "Population decline"), [counterurbanisation](/wiki/Counterurbanisation "Counterurbanisation"), the collapse of centralised authority, invasions, and mass migrations of [tribes](/wiki/Tribe "Tribe") had begun in [late antiquity](/wiki/Late_antiquity "Late antiquity") (c. ~3rd–8th centuries) and lasted into the Early Middle Ages (c. 5th–10th centuries). The large-scale movements of the [Migration Period](/wiki/Migration_Period "Migration Period"), including various [Germanic peoples](/wiki/Germanic_peoples "Germanic peoples"), formed new kingdoms in what remained of the Western Roman Empire. In the 7th century, [North Africa](/wiki/North_Africa "North Africa") and the Middle East—once part of the [Byzantine Empire](/wiki/Byzantine_Empire "Byzantine Empire")—came under the rule of the [Umayyad Caliphate](/wiki/Umayyad_Caliphate "Umayyad Caliphate"), an Islamic empire, after conquest by [Muhammad's successors](/wiki/Succession_to_Muhammad "Succession to Muhammad"). Although there were substantial changes in society and political structures, the break with [classical antiquity](/wiki/Classical_antiquity "Classical antiquity") was incomplete. The still-sizeable Byzantine Empire, Rome's direct continuation, survived in the Eastern Mediterranean and remained a major power. The empire's law code, the *[Corpus Juris Civilis](/wiki/Corpus_Juris_Civilis "Corpus Juris Civilis")* or "Code of Justinian", was rediscovered in [Northern Italy](/wiki/Northern_Italy "Northern Italy") in the 11th century. In the West, most kingdoms incorporated the few extant Roman institutions. [Monasteries](/wiki/Monastery "Monastery") were founded as campaigns to [Christianise](/wiki/Christianise "Christianise") the [remaining pagans across Europe](/wiki/Paganism#Ethnic_religions_of_pre-Christian_Europe "Paganism") continued. The [Franks](/wiki/Franks "Franks"), under the [Carolingian dynasty](/wiki/Carolingian_dynasty "Carolingian dynasty"), briefly established the [Carolingian Empire](/wiki/Carolingian_Empire "Carolingian Empire") during the later 8th and early 9th centuries. It covered much of Western Europe but later succumbed to the pressures of internal civil wars combined with external invasions: [Vikings](/wiki/Vikings "Vikings") from the north, [Magyars](/wiki/Hungarian_invasions_of_Europe "Hungarian invasions of Europe") from the east, and [Saracens](/wiki/Saracen "Saracen") from the south.

During the High Middle Ages, which began after 1000, the population of Europe increased significantly as technological and [agricultural innovations](/wiki/Agriculture_in_the_Middle_Ages#Technological_innovation "Agriculture in the Middle Ages") allowed trade to flourish and the [Medieval Warm Period](/wiki/Medieval_Warm_Period "Medieval Warm Period") climate change allowed crop yields to increase. [Manorialism](/wiki/Manorialism "Manorialism"), the organisation of [peasants](/wiki/Peasant "Peasant") into villages that owed rent and labour services to the [nobles](/wiki/Nobles "Nobles"), and [feudalism](/wiki/Feudalism "Feudalism"), the political structure whereby [knights](/wiki/Knight "Knight") and lower-status nobles owed military service to their [overlords](/wiki/Overlord "Overlord") in return for the right to rent from lands and [manors](/wiki/Manorialism "Manorialism"), were two of the ways society was organised in the High Middle Ages. This period also saw the collapse of the unified Christian church with the [East–West Schism](/wiki/East%E2%80%93West_Schism "East–West Schism") of 1054. The [Crusades](/wiki/Crusades "Crusades"), first preached in 1095, were military attempts by Western European Christians to regain control of the [Holy Land](/wiki/Holy_Land "Holy Land") from [Muslims](/wiki/Muslim "Muslim"). Kings became the heads of centralised [nation-states](/wiki/Nation-states "Nation-states"), reducing crime and violence but making the ideal of a unified [Christendom](/wiki/Christendom "Christendom") more distant. Intellectual life was marked by [scholasticism](/wiki/Scholasticism "Scholasticism"), a philosophy that emphasised joining faith to reason, and by the founding of [universities](/wiki/Universities "Universities"). The theology of [Thomas Aquinas](/wiki/Thomas_Aquinas "Thomas Aquinas"), the paintings of [Giotto](/wiki/Giotto "Giotto"), the poetry of [Dante](/wiki/Dante "Dante") and [Chaucer](/wiki/Chaucer "Chaucer"), the travels of [Marco Polo](/wiki/Marco_Polo "Marco Polo"), and the [Gothic architecture](/wiki/Gothic_architecture "Gothic architecture") of cathedrals such as [Chartres](/wiki/Chartres_Cathedral "Chartres Cathedral") are among the outstanding achievements toward the end of this period and into the Late Middle Ages.

The Late Middle Ages was marked by difficulties and calamities, including famine, plague, and war, which significantly diminished the population of Europe; between 1347 and 1350, the [Black Death](/wiki/Black_Death "Black Death") killed about a third of Europeans. Controversy, [heresy](/wiki/Heresy "Heresy"), and the [Western Schism](/wiki/Western_Schism "Western Schism") within the [Catholic Church](/wiki/Catholic_Church "Catholic Church") paralleled the interstate conflict, civil strife, and [peasant revolts](/wiki/Popular_revolts_in_late-medieval_Europe "Popular revolts in late-medieval Europe") that occurred in the kingdoms. Cultural and technological developments transformed European society, concluding the Late Middle Ages and beginning the [early modern period](/wiki/Early_modern_period "Early modern period").

## Terminology and periodisation

The Middle Ages is one of the three major periods in the most enduring scheme for analysing [European history](/wiki/European_history "European history"): [classical civilisation](/wiki/Classical_civilisation "Classical civilisation") or [antiquity](/wiki/Ancient_history "Ancient history"), the Middle Ages and the [modern period](/wiki/Modern_Period "Modern Period").[[1]](#cite_note-Power304-1) The "Middle Ages" first appears in Latin in 1469 as *media tempestas* or "middle season".[[2]](#cite_note-Miglio112-2) In early usage, there were many variants, including *medium aevum*, or "middle age", first recorded in 1604,[[3]](#cite_note-Albrow205-3) and *media saecula*, or "middle centuries", first recorded in 1625.[[4]](#cite_note-Murray4-4) The adjective "medieval" (or sometimes "mediaeval"[[5]](#cite_note-Random1194-5) or "mediæval"),[[6]](#cite_note-OED290-6) meaning pertaining to the Middle Ages, derives from *medium aevum*.[[5]](#cite_note-Random1194-5)

Medieval writers divided history into periods such as the "[Six Ages](/wiki/Six_Ages "Six Ages")" or the "[Four Empires](/wiki/Four_Empires "Four Empires")" and considered their time to be the last before the end of the world.[[7]](#cite_note-mommsen236-7) When referring to their own times, they spoke of them as being "modern".[[8]](#cite_note-Dailyx-8) In the 1330s, the Italian humanist and poet [Petrarch](/wiki/Petrarch "Petrarch") referred to pre-Christian times as *antiqua* ('ancient') and to the Christian period as *nova* ('new').[[9]](#cite_note-idea-9) Petrarch regarded the post-Roman centuries as "[dark](/wiki/Dark_Ages_(historiography) "Dark Ages (historiography)")" compared to the "light" of [classical antiquity](/wiki/Classical_antiquity "Classical antiquity").[[10]](#cite_note-Mommsen227-10) [Leonardo Bruni](/wiki/Leonardo_Bruni "Leonardo Bruni") was the first historian to use [tripartite periodisation](/wiki/Late_Middle_Ages#Historiography_and_periodization "Late Middle Ages") in his *History of the Florentine People* (1442), with a middle period "between the fall of the Roman Empire and the revival of city life sometime in late eleventh and twelfth centuries".[[11]](#cite_note-Brunixvii-11) Tripartite [periodisation](/wiki/Periodisation "Periodisation") became standard after the 17th-century German historian [Christoph Cellarius](/wiki/Christoph_Cellarius "Christoph Cellarius") divided history into three periods: ancient, medieval, and modern.[[4]](#cite_note-Murray4-4)

The most commonly given starting point for the Middle Ages is around 500,[[12]](#cite_note-12) with the date of 476 first used by Bruni--mapping to the removal of the [last (native) Roman emperor](/wiki/Romulus_Augustulus "Romulus Augustulus") in the West from power and his replacement by [a German chieftain](/wiki/Odoacer "Odoacer").[[11]](#cite_note-Brunixvii-11)[[A]](#cite_note-14) Later starting dates are sometimes used in the outer parts of Europe.[[14]](#cite_note-15) For Europe as a whole, 1500 is often considered to be the end of the Middle Ages,[[15]](#cite_note-16) but there is no universally agreed upon end date. Depending on the context, events such as the [conquest of Constantinople](/wiki/Conquest_of_Constantinople "Conquest of Constantinople") by the Turks in 1453, [Christopher Columbus](/wiki/Christopher_Columbus "Christopher Columbus")'s [first voyage](/wiki/Voyages_of_Christopher_Columbus#First_voyage_(1492–1493) "Voyages of Christopher Columbus") to the [Americas](/wiki/Americas "Americas") in 1492, or the [Reformation](/wiki/Reformation "Reformation") in 1517 are sometimes used.[[16]](#cite_note-Davies291-17) English historians often use the [Battle of Bosworth Field](/wiki/Battle_of_Bosworth_Field "Battle of Bosworth Field") in 1485 to mark the end of the period.[[17]](#cite_note-18) For Spain, dates commonly used are the death of King [Ferdinand II](/wiki/Ferdinand_II_of_Aragon "Ferdinand II of Aragon") in 1516, the death of Queen [Isabella I of Castile](/wiki/Isabella_I_of_Castile "Isabella I of Castile") in 1504, or the [conquest of Granada](/wiki/Conquest_of_Granada "Conquest of Granada") in 1492.[[18]](#cite_note-19) 1648, marking the end of [the European wars of religion](/wiki/European_wars_of_religion "European wars of religion"), might be considered an upper-bound that preserves the tripartite schema of the terminology (classical antiquity, the medieval, and the modern).[[19]](#cite_note-20) [The European Renaissance](/wiki/Renaissance "Renaissance"), [the Reformation,](/wiki/Reformation "Reformation") and [Early Modernity](/wiki/Early_modern_period "Early modern period") would, in that case, all refer to a transition between the Middle Ages and modernity in Western Europe instead of being counted as their own periods, similar to the way that the fall of the Western Roman Empire (occuring over a period of centuries) marks the beginning of the medieval period. By the end of the Thirty Years War, this transition has been accomplished in a [geopolitical](/wiki/Geopolitics "Geopolitics") sense and the territory previously known as [Christendom](/wiki/Christendom "Christendom") begins to be called [Europe](/wiki/Europe "Europe").

Historians from [Romance-speaking](/wiki/Romance-speaking "Romance-speaking") countries tend to divide the Middle Ages into two parts: an earlier "High" and later "Low" period. English-speaking historians, following their German counterparts, generally subdivide the Middle Ages into three intervals: "Early", "High", and "Late".[[1]](#cite_note-Power304-1) In the 19th century, the entire Middle Ages were often referred to as the "[Dark Ages](/wiki/Dark_Ages_(Europe) "Dark Ages (Europe)")", but with the adoption of these subdivisions, use of this term was restricted to the Early Middle Ages, at least among historians.[[20]](#cite_note-mommsen226-21)

## Later Roman Empire

Main article: [Later Roman Empire](/wiki/Later_Roman_Empire "Later Roman Empire")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/72/Venice_city_scenes_-_in_St._Mark%27s_square_-_St_Mark%27s_Basilica_%2811002237996%29.jpg/250px-Venice_city_scenes_-_in_St._Mark%27s_square_-_St_Mark%27s_Basilica_%2811002237996%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Venice_city_scenes_-_in_St._Mark%27s_square_-_St_Mark%27s_Basilica_(11002237996).jpg)

A late Roman sculpture [depicting the four Tetrarchs](/wiki/Portrait_of_the_Four_Tetrarchs "Portrait of the Four Tetrarchs"), now in [Venice](/wiki/Venice "Venice"), Italy[[21]](#cite_note-Tansey242-22)

The [Roman Empire](/wiki/Roman_Empire "Roman Empire") reached its greatest territorial extent during the 2nd century AD; the following two centuries witnessed the slow decline of Roman control over its outlying territories.[[22]](#cite_note-Cunliffe391-23) Economic issues, including inflation, and external pressure on the frontiers combined to create the [Crisis of the Third Century](/wiki/Crisis_of_the_Third_Century "Crisis of the Third Century"), with emperors coming to the throne only to be rapidly replaced by new usurpers.[[23]](#cite_note-Collins3-24) Military expenses increased steadily during the 3rd century, mainly in response to the [war](/wiki/Roman%E2%80%93Persian_Wars#Roman–Sassanid_War "Roman–Persian Wars") with the [Sasanian Empire](/wiki/Sasanian_Empire "Sasanian Empire"), which revived in the middle of the 3rd century.[[24]](#cite_note-Heather111-25) The army doubled in size, and cavalry and smaller units replaced the [Roman legion](/wiki/Roman_legion "Roman legion") as the main tactical unit.[[25]](#cite_note-Brown24-25-26) The need for revenue led to increased taxes and a decline in numbers of the [curial](/wiki/Curiales "Curiales"), or landowning, class, and decreasing numbers of them willing to shoulder the burdens of holding office in their native towns.[[24]](#cite_note-Heather111-25) More bureaucrats were needed in the central administration to deal with the needs of the army, which led to complaints from civilians that there were more tax-collectors in the empire than tax-payers.[[25]](#cite_note-Brown24-25-26)

The Emperor [Diocletian](/wiki/Diocletian "Diocletian") (r. 284–305) split the empire into separately administered [eastern](/wiki/Byzantine_Empire "Byzantine Empire") and [western](/wiki/Western_Roman_Empire "Western Roman Empire") halves in 286; the empire was not considered divided by its inhabitants or rulers, as legal and administrative [promulgations](/wiki/Promulgation "Promulgation") in one division were considered valid in the other.[[26]](#cite_note-Collins9-27)[[B]](#cite_note-28) In 330, after a period of civil war, [Constantine the Great](/wiki/Constantine_the_Great "Constantine the Great") (r. 306–337) refounded the city of [Byzantium](/wiki/Byzantium "Byzantium") as the newly renamed eastern capital, [Constantinople](/wiki/Constantinople "Constantinople").[[27]](#cite_note-Collins24-29) Diocletian's reforms strengthened the governmental bureaucracy, reformed taxation, and strengthened the army, which bought the empire time but did not resolve the problems it was facing: excessive taxation, a declining birthrate, and pressures on its frontiers, among others.[[28]](#cite_note-Cunliffe405-30) Civil war between rival emperors became common in the middle of the 4th century, diverting soldiers from the empire's frontier forces and allowing [invaders](/wiki/Barbarian#Modern_academia "Barbarian") to encroach.[[29]](#cite_note-Collins31-31) For much of the 4th century, Roman society stabilised in a new form that differed from the earlier [classical period](/wiki/Culture_of_ancient_Rome "Culture of ancient Rome"), with a widening gulf between the rich and poor, and a decline in the vitality of the smaller towns.[[30]](#cite_note-Brown34-32) Another change was the [Christianisation](/wiki/Christianisation "Christianisation"), or conversion of the empire to [Christianity](/wiki/Christianity "Christianity"), a gradual process that lasted from the 2nd to the 5th centuries.[[31]](#cite_note-Brown65-33)[[32]](#cite_note-Brown82-34)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/66/Europe_map_450.PNG/500px-Europe_map_450.PNG)](/wiki/File:Europe_map_450.PNG)

Map of the approximate political boundaries in Europe around 450 AD

In 376, the [Goths](/wiki/Goths "Goths"), fleeing from the [Huns](/wiki/Huns "Huns"), received permission from Emperor [Valens](/wiki/Valens "Valens") (r. 364–378) to settle in the Roman province of [Thracia](/wiki/Thracia "Thracia") in the [Balkans](/wiki/Balkans "Balkans"). The settlement did not go smoothly, and the Goths began to raid and plunder when Roman officials mishandled the situation.[[C]](#cite_note-36) Valens, attempting to put down the disorder, was killed fighting the Goths at the [Battle of Adrianople](/wiki/Battle_of_Adrianople "Battle of Adrianople") on 9 August 378.[[34]](#cite_note-Bauer47-37) In addition to the threat from such tribal confederacies in the north, internal divisions within the empire, especially within the Christian Church, caused problems.[[35]](#cite_note-Bauer56-38) In 400, the [Visigoths](/wiki/Visigoths "Visigoths") invaded the Western Roman Empire and, although briefly forced back from Italy, in 410 [sacked the city of Rome](/wiki/Sack_of_Rome_(410) "Sack of Rome (410)").[[36]](#cite_note-Bauer80-39) In 406 the [Alans](/wiki/Alans "Alans"), [Vandals](/wiki/Vandals "Vandals"), and [Suevi](/wiki/Suevi "Suevi") crossed into [Gaul](/wiki/Gaul "Gaul"); over the next three years they spread across Gaul and in 409 crossed the [Pyrenees Mountains](/wiki/Pyrenees_Mountains "Pyrenees Mountains") into modern-day Spain.[[37]](#cite_note-Collins59-40) The [Migration Period](/wiki/Migration_Period "Migration Period") began, when various peoples, initially largely [Germanic peoples](/wiki/Germanic_peoples "Germanic peoples"), moved across Europe. The [Franks](/wiki/Franks "Franks"), [Alemanni](/wiki/Alemanni "Alemanni"), and the [Burgundians](/wiki/Burgundians "Burgundians") all ended up in northern Gaul while the [Angles](/wiki/Angles_(tribe) "Angles (tribe)"), [Saxons](/wiki/Saxons "Saxons"), and [Jutes](/wiki/Jutes "Jutes") [settled in Britain](/wiki/Anglo-Saxon_settlement_of_Britain "Anglo-Saxon settlement of Britain"),[[38]](#cite_note-Cunliffe417-41) and the Vandals went on to cross the strait of Gibraltar after which they conquered the province of [Africa](/wiki/Africa_(Roman_province) "Africa (Roman province)").[[39]](#cite_note-Collins80-42) In the 430s the Huns began invading the empire; their king [Attila](/wiki/Attila "Attila") (r. 434–453) led invasions into the Balkans in 442 and 447, Gaul in 451, and Italy in 452.[[40]](#cite_note-James67-43) The Hunnic threat remained until Attila's death in 453, when the [Hunnic confederation](/wiki/Huns#Unified_Empire_under_Attila "Huns") he led fell apart.[[41]](#cite_note-Bauer117-44) These invasions by the tribes completely changed the political and demographic nature of what had been the Western Roman Empire.[[38]](#cite_note-Cunliffe417-41)

By the end of the 5th century, the western section of the empire was divided into smaller political units ruled by the tribes that had invaded in the early part of the century.[[42]](#cite_note-Wickham79-45) The deposition of the last emperor of the west, [Romulus Augustulus](/wiki/Romulus_Augustulus "Romulus Augustulus"), in 476 has traditionally marked the end of the Western Roman Empire.[[13]](#cite_note-Wickham86-13)[[D]](#cite_note-46) By 493 the Italian peninsula was conquered by the [Ostrogoths](/wiki/Ostrogoths "Ostrogoths").[[43]](#cite_note-Collins107-47) The Eastern Roman Empire, often referred to as the Byzantine Empire after the fall of its western counterpart, had little ability to assert control over the lost western territories. The [Byzantine emperors](/wiki/Byzantine_emperors "Byzantine emperors") maintained a claim over the territory, but while none of the new kings in the west dared to elevate himself to the position of emperor of the west, Byzantine control of most of the Western Empire could not be sustained; the reconquest of the Mediterranean periphery and the [Italian Peninsula](/wiki/Italian_Peninsula "Italian Peninsula") ([Gothic War](/wiki/Gothic_War_(535%E2%80%93554) "Gothic War (535–554)")) in the reign of [Justinian](/wiki/Justinian "Justinian") (r. 527–565) was the sole, and temporary, exception.[[44]](#cite_note-Collins116-48)

## Early Middle Ages

Main article: [Early Middle Ages](/wiki/Early_Middle_Ages "Early Middle Ages")

### New societies

Main articles: [Migration Period](/wiki/Migration_Period "Migration Period") and [fall of the Western Roman Empire](/wiki/Fall_of_the_Western_Roman_Empire "Fall of the Western Roman Empire")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0b/Europe_and_the_Near_East_at_476_AD.png/330px-Europe_and_the_Near_East_at_476_AD.png)](/wiki/File:Europe_and_the_Near_East_at_476_AD.png)

[Barbarian kingdoms](/wiki/Barbarian_kingdoms "Barbarian kingdoms") and tribes after the end of the Western Roman Empire

The political structure of Western Europe changed with the end of the united Roman Empire. Although the movements of peoples during this period are usually described as "invasions", they were not just military expeditions but migrations of entire peoples into the empire. Such movements were aided by the refusal of the Western Roman elites to support the army or pay the taxes that would have allowed the military to suppress the migration.[[45]](#cite_note-Brown122-49) The emperors of the 5th century were often controlled by military strongmen such as [Stilicho](/wiki/Stilicho "Stilicho") (d. 408), [Aetius](/wiki/Flavius_Aetius "Flavius Aetius") (d. 454), [Aspar](/wiki/Aspar "Aspar") (d. 471), [Ricimer](/wiki/Ricimer "Ricimer") (d. 472), or [Gundobad](/wiki/Gundobad "Gundobad") (d. 516), who were partly or fully of non-Roman background. When the line of Western emperors ceased, many of the kings who replaced them were from the same background. Intermarriage between the new kings and the Roman elites was common.[[46]](#cite_note-Wickham95-50) This led to a fusion of Roman culture with the customs of the invading tribes, including the popular assemblies that allowed free male tribal members more say in political matters than was common in the Roman state.[[47]](#cite_note-Wickham100-51) Material artefacts left by the Romans and the invaders are often similar, and tribal items were often modelled on Roman objects.[[48]](#cite_note-Collins100-52) Much of the scholarly and written culture of the new kingdoms was also based on Roman intellectual traditions.[[49]](#cite_note-Collins96-53) An important difference was the new polities' gradual loss of tax revenue. Many new political entities no longer supported their armies through taxes; instead, they relied on granting them land or rents. This meant there was less need for large tax revenues, so the [taxation systems](/wiki/Roman_Empire#Taxation "Roman Empire") decayed.[[50]](#cite_note-Wickham102-54) Warfare was common between and within the kingdoms. Slavery declined as the supply weakened, and society became more rural.[[51]](#cite_note-Backman86-55)[[E]](#cite_note-57)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/54/Theoderic_Quarter_Siliqua_80000847.jpg/250px-Theoderic_Quarter_Siliqua_80000847.jpg)](/wiki/File:Theoderic_Quarter_Siliqua_80000847.jpg)

A coin of the [Ostrogothic](/wiki/Ostrogoths "Ostrogoths") leader [Theoderic the Great](/wiki/Theoderic_the_Great "Theoderic the Great"), struck in Milan, Italy, c. AD 491–501

Between the 5th and 8th centuries, new peoples and individuals filled the political void left by the centralised Roman government.[[49]](#cite_note-Collins96-53) The [Ostrogoths](/wiki/Ostrogoths "Ostrogoths"), a Gothic tribe, settled in [Roman Italy](/wiki/Roman_Italy "Roman Italy") in the late fifth century under [Theoderic the Great](/wiki/Theoderic_the_Great "Theoderic the Great") (d. 526) and set up a [kingdom](/wiki/Ostrogothic_Kingdom "Ostrogothic Kingdom") marked by its co-operation between the Italians and the Ostrogoths, at least until the last years of Theodoric's reign.[[53]](#cite_note-James82-58) The Burgundians settled in Gaul, and after an earlier realm was destroyed by the Huns in 436, formed a new kingdom in the 440s. Between today's [Geneva](/wiki/Geneva "Geneva") and [Lyon](/wiki/Lyon "Lyon"), it grew to become the realm of [Burgundy](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Burgundy#First_Kingdom_of_Burgundy_(4th_century_–_534_AD) "Kingdom of Burgundy") in the late 5th and early 6th centuries.[[54]](#cite_note-James77-59) Elsewhere in Gaul, the Franks and [Celtic Britons](/wiki/Celtic_Britons "Celtic Britons") set up small polities. [Francia](/wiki/Francia "Francia") was centred in northern Gaul, and the first king of whom much is known is [Childeric I](/wiki/Childeric_I "Childeric I") (d. 481). His grave was discovered in 1653 and is remarkable for its [grave goods](/wiki/Grave_goods "Grave goods"), which included weapons and a large quantity of gold.[[55]](#cite_note-James79-60)

Under Childeric's son [Clovis I](/wiki/Clovis_I "Clovis I") (r. 509–511), the founder of the [Merovingian dynasty](/wiki/Merovingian_dynasty "Merovingian dynasty"), the Frankish kingdom expanded and converted to Christianity. The Britons, related to the natives of [Britannia](/wiki/Britannia "Britannia") – modern-day Great Britain – settled in what is now [Brittany](/wiki/Brittany "Brittany").[[56]](#cite_note-James78-61)[[F]](#cite_note-62) Other monarchies were established by the [Visigothic Kingdom](/wiki/Visigothic_Kingdom "Visigothic Kingdom") in the [Iberian Peninsula](/wiki/Iberian_Peninsula "Iberian Peninsula"), the [Suebi](/wiki/Suebi "Suebi") in northwestern Iberia, and the [Vandal Kingdom](/wiki/Vandal_Kingdom "Vandal Kingdom") in [North Africa](/wiki/North_Africa "North Africa").[[54]](#cite_note-James77-59) In the sixth century, the [Lombards](/wiki/Lombards "Lombards") settled in [Northern Italy](/wiki/Northern_Italy "Northern Italy"), replacing the Ostrogothic kingdom with a grouping of [duchies](/wiki/Duchies "Duchies") that occasionally selected a king to rule over them all. By the late sixth century, this arrangement had been replaced by a permanent monarchy, the [Kingdom of the Lombards](/wiki/Kingdom_of_the_Lombards "Kingdom of the Lombards").[[57]](#cite_note-Collins196-63)

The invasions brought new ethnic groups to Europe, although some regions received a larger influx of new peoples than others. In Gaul, for instance, the invaders settled much more extensively in the north-east than in the south-west. [Slavs](/wiki/Slavs "Slavs") settled in [Central](/wiki/Central_Europe "Central Europe") and [Eastern Europe](/wiki/Eastern_Europe "Eastern Europe") and the Balkan Peninsula. Changes in languages accompanied the settlement of peoples. [Latin](/wiki/Latin "Latin"), the literary language of the Western Roman Empire, was gradually replaced by [vernacular languages](/wiki/Vernacular_languages "Vernacular languages"), which evolved from Latin but were distinct from it, collectively known as [Romance languages](/wiki/Romance_languages "Romance languages"). These changes from Latin to the new languages took many centuries. Greek remained the language of the Byzantine Empire, but the migrations of the Slavs added [Slavic languages](/wiki/Slavic_languages "Slavic languages") to Eastern Europe.[[58]](#cite_note-Davies235-64)

### Byzantine survival

Main articles: [Byzantine Empire under the Justinian dynasty](/wiki/Byzantine_Empire_under_the_Justinian_dynasty "Byzantine Empire under the Justinian dynasty") and [Byzantine Empire under the Heraclian dynasty](/wiki/Byzantine_Empire_under_the_Heraclian_dynasty "Byzantine Empire under the Heraclian dynasty")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2c/Sanvitale03.jpg/250px-Sanvitale03.jpg)](/wiki/File:Sanvitale03.jpg)

A [mosaic](/wiki/Mosaic "Mosaic") showing [Justinian](/wiki/Justinian "Justinian") with [the bishop](/wiki/Maximianus_of_Ravenna "Maximianus of Ravenna") of [Ravenna](/wiki/Ravenna "Ravenna") (Italy), bodyguards, and courtiers.[[59]](#cite_note-Adams158-65)

As Western Europe witnessed the formation of new kingdoms, the Eastern Roman Empire remained intact and experienced an economic revival that lasted into the early 7th century. There were fewer invasions of the eastern section of the empire; most occurred in the Balkans. Peace with the [Sasanian Empire](/wiki/Sasanian_Empire "Sasanian Empire"), Rome's traditional enemy, lasted most of the 5th century. The Eastern Empire was marked by closer relations between the political state and the Christian Church, with doctrinal matters assuming an importance in Eastern politics that they did not have in Western Europe. Legal developments included the codification of [Roman law](/wiki/Roman_law "Roman law"); the first effort—the *[Codex Theodosianus](/wiki/Codex_Theodosianus "Codex Theodosianus")*—was completed in 438.[[60]](#cite_note-Wickham81-66) Under Emperor Justinian (r. 527–565), another compilation took place—the *[Corpus Juris Civilis](/wiki/Corpus_Juris_Civilis "Corpus Juris Civilis")*.[[61]](#cite_note-Bauer200-67) Justinian also oversaw the construction of the [Hagia Sophia](/wiki/Hagia_Sophia "Hagia Sophia") in Constantinople and the reconquest of North Africa from the Vandals and Italy from the Ostrogoths,[[62]](#cite_note-Bauer206-68) under [Belisarius](/wiki/Belisarius "Belisarius") (d. 565).[[63]](#cite_note-Collins126-69) The conquest of Italy was not complete, as a deadly outbreak of [plague in 542](/wiki/Plague_of_Justinian "Plague of Justinian") led to the rest of Justinian's reign concentrating on defensive measures rather than further conquests.[[62]](#cite_note-Bauer206-68)

At the Emperor's death, the Byzantines had control of [most of Italy](/wiki/Exarchate_of_Ravenna "Exarchate of Ravenna"), North Africa, and a small foothold in southern Spain. Historians have criticised Justinian's reconquests for overextending his realm and setting the stage for the [early Muslim conquests](/wiki/Early_Muslim_conquests "Early Muslim conquests"), but many of the difficulties faced by Justinian's successors were due not just to over-taxation to pay for his wars but to the essentially civilian nature of the empire, which made raising troops difficult.[[64]](#cite_note-Brown8-70)

In the Eastern Empire, the Slavs' slow infiltration of the Balkans added further difficulty for Justinian's successors. It began gradually, but by the late 540s, Slavic tribes were in [Thrace](/wiki/Thrace "Thrace") and [Illyrium](/wiki/Illyricum_(Roman_province) "Illyricum (Roman province)") and had defeated an imperial army near [Adrianople](/wiki/Edirne "Edirne") in 551. In the 560s, the [Avars](/wiki/Pannonian_Avars "Pannonian Avars") began to expand from their base on the north bank of the [Danube](/wiki/Danube "Danube"); by the end of the 6th century, they were the dominant power in Central Europe and routinely able to force the Eastern emperors to pay tribute. They remained a strong power until 796.[[65]](#cite_note-James95-71)

An additional problem to face the empire came as a result of the involvement of Emperor [Maurice](/wiki/Maurice_(emperor) "Maurice (emperor)") (r. 582–602) in Persian politics when he intervened in a [succession dispute](/wiki/Khosrow_II#Accession_to_the_throne "Khosrow II"). This led to a period of peace, but when Maurice was overthrown, [the Persians invaded](/wiki/Byzantine%E2%80%93Sasanian_War_of_602%E2%80%93628 "Byzantine–Sasanian War of 602–628") and during the reign of Emperor [Heraclius](/wiki/Heraclius "Heraclius") (r. 610–641) controlled large chunks of the empire, including Egypt, Syria, and [Anatolia](/wiki/Anatolia "Anatolia") until Heraclius' successful counterattack. In 628, the empire secured a peace treaty and recovered its lost territories.[[66]](#cite_note-Collins140-72)

### Western society

See also: [Early medieval European dress](/wiki/Early_medieval_European_dress "Early medieval European dress") and [medieval cuisine](/wiki/Medieval_cuisine "Medieval cuisine")

In Western Europe, some older Roman elite families died out while others became more involved with ecclesiastical than secular affairs. Values attached to [Latin scholarship](/wiki/Latin_literature "Latin literature") and [education](/wiki/Education_in_Ancient_Rome "Education in Ancient Rome") mostly disappeared, and while literacy remained important, it became a practical skill rather than a sign of elite status. In the 4th century, [Jerome](/wiki/Jerome "Jerome") (d. 420) dreamed that God rebuked him for spending more time reading [Cicero](/wiki/Cicero "Cicero") than the [Bible](/wiki/Bible "Bible"). By the 6th century, [Gregory of Tours](/wiki/Gregory_of_Tours "Gregory of Tours") (d. 594) had a similar dream, but instead of being chastised for reading Cicero, he was chastised for learning [shorthand](/wiki/Shorthand "Shorthand").[[67]](#cite_note-Brown174-73) By the late 6th century, the principal means of religious instruction in the Church had become music and art rather than the book.[[68]](#cite_note-Brown181-74) Most intellectual efforts went towards imitating classical scholarship, but some [original works](/wiki/Early_medieval_literature "Early medieval literature") were created, along with now-lost oral compositions. The writings of [Sidonius Apollinaris](/wiki/Sidonius_Apollinaris "Sidonius Apollinaris") (d. 489), [Cassiodorus](/wiki/Cassiodorus "Cassiodorus") (d. c. 585), and [Boethius](/wiki/Boethius "Boethius") (d. c. 525) were typical of the age.[[69]](#cite_note-Brown45-75)

Changes also occurred among laypeople, as aristocratic culture focused on great feasts held in halls rather than on literary pursuits. Clothing for the elites was richly embellished with jewels and gold. Lords and kings supported the entourages of fighters who formed the backbone of the military forces.[[G]](#cite_note-78) Family ties within the elites were important, as were the virtues of loyalty, courage, and honour. These ties led to the prevalence of feuds in aristocratic society, including those related by Gregory of Tours in [Merovingian](/wiki/Merovingian "Merovingian") Gaul. Most feuds seem to have ended quickly with the payment of some [compensation](/wiki/Weregild "Weregild").[[72]](#cite_note-Wickham189-79) Women took part in aristocratic society mainly in their roles as wives and mothers of men, with the role of mother of a ruler being especially prominent in Merovingian Gaul. In [Anglo-Saxon](/wiki/Anglo-Saxon "Anglo-Saxon") society, the lack of many child rulers meant a lesser role for women as queen mothers, but this was compensated for by the increased role played by [abbesses](/wiki/Abbess "Abbess") of monasteries. Only in Italy does it appear that women were always considered under the protection and control of a male relative.[[73]](#cite_note-Wickham195-80)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9a/Fr%C3%BChmittelalterliches_Dorf.jpg/250px-Fr%C3%BChmittelalterliches_Dorf.jpg)](/wiki/File:Fr%C3%BChmittelalterliches_Dorf.jpg)

Reconstruction of an early medieval peasant village in Bavaria, Germany

Peasant society is much less documented than the nobility. Most of the surviving information available to historians comes from [archaeology](/wiki/Archaeology "Archaeology"); few detailed written records documenting peasant life remain from before the 9th century. Most of the descriptions of the lower classes come from either [law codes](/wiki/Code_(law) "Code (law)") or writers from the upper classes.[[74]](#cite_note-Wickham204-81) [Landholding](/wiki/Landholding "Landholding") patterns in the West were not uniform; some areas had greatly fragmented landholding patterns, but in other areas, large contiguous blocks of land were the norm. These differences allowed for a wide variety of peasant societies, some dominated by aristocratic landholders and others having great autonomy.[[75]](#cite_note-Wickham205-82) Land settlement also varied greatly. Some peasants lived in large settlements that numbered as many as 700 inhabitants. Others lived in small groups of a few families and lived on isolated farms spread over the countryside. There were also areas where the pattern was a mix of two or more systems.[[76]](#cite_note-Wickham211-83) Unlike in the late Roman period, there was no sharp break between the legal status of the free peasant and the aristocrat, and a free peasant's family could rise into the aristocracy over several generations through military service to a powerful lord.[[77]](#cite_note-Wickham215-84)

Roman city life and culture changed greatly in the early Middle Ages. Although Italian cities remained inhabited, they contracted significantly in size. For instance, Rome shrank from hundreds of thousands to around 30,000 by the end of the 6th century. [Roman temples](/wiki/Roman_temple "Roman temple") were converted into [Christian churches](/wiki/Christianised_sites "Christianised sites") and city walls remained in use.[[78]](#cite_note-Brown24-26-85) In Northern Europe, cities also shrank, while civic monuments and other public buildings were raided for building materials. The establishment of new kingdoms often meant some growth for the towns chosen as capitals.[[79]](#cite_note-City3-86) Although there had been [Jewish communities in many Roman cities](/wiki/Jews_in_the_Roman_Empire "Jews in the Roman Empire"), the [Jews](/wiki/Jews "Jews") suffered periods of persecution after the conversion of the empire to Christianity. Officially, they were tolerated, if subject to conversion efforts, and were sometimes encouraged to settle in new areas.[[80]](#cite_note-Jews191-87)

### Rise of Islam

Main articles: [Spread of Islam](/wiki/Spread_of_Islam "Spread of Islam") and [Early Muslim conquests](/wiki/Early_Muslim_conquests "Early Muslim conquests")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/72/Map_of_expansion_of_Caliphate.svg/330px-Map_of_expansion_of_Caliphate.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Map_of_expansion_of_Caliphate.svg)

The [early Muslim conquests](/wiki/Early_Muslim_conquests "Early Muslim conquests")

Expansion under Muhammad, 622–632

Expansion during the Rashidun Caliphate, 632–661

Expansion during the Umayyad Caliphate, 661–750

Religious beliefs in the Eastern Roman Empire and Iran were in flux during the late sixth and early seventh centuries. [Judaism](/wiki/Judaism "Judaism") was an active proselytising faith, and at least one [Arab](/wiki/Arab "Arab") political leader converted to it.[[H]](#cite_note-89) In addition Jewish theologians wrote polemics defending their religion against Christian and Islamic influences.[[82]](#cite_note-90)

Christianity had active missions competing with the Persians' [Zoroastrianism](/wiki/Zoroastrianism "Zoroastrianism") in seeking converts, especially among residents of the [Arabian Peninsula](/wiki/Arabian_Peninsula "Arabian Peninsula"). All these strands came together with the emergence of [Islam](/wiki/Islam "Islam") in Arabia during the lifetime of [Muhammad](/wiki/Muhammad "Muhammad") (d. 632).[[83]](#cite_note-Collins143-91) After his death, Islamic forces conquered much of the Eastern Roman Empire and Persia, starting with [Syria](/wiki/Muslim_conquest_of_the_Levant "Muslim conquest of the Levant") in 634–635, continuing with [Persia](/wiki/Muslim_conquest_of_Persia "Muslim conquest of Persia") between 637 and 642, reaching [Egypt](/wiki/Muslim_conquest_of_Egypt "Muslim conquest of Egypt") in 640–641, [North Africa](/wiki/Muslim_conquest_of_the_Maghreb "Muslim conquest of the Maghreb") in the later seventh century, and the [Iberian Peninsula](/wiki/Umayyad_conquest_of_Hispania "Umayyad conquest of Hispania") in 711.[[84]](#cite_note-Collins149-92) By 714, Islamic forces controlled much of the peninsula in a region they called [Al-Andalus](/wiki/Al-Andalus "Al-Andalus").[[85]](#cite_note-Reilly52-93)

The Islamic conquests reached their peak in the mid-eighth century. The defeat of Muslim forces at the [Battle of Tours](/wiki/Battle_of_Tours "Battle of Tours") in 732 led to the reconquest of southern France by the Franks, but the main reason for the halt of Islamic growth in Europe was the overthrow of the [Umayyad Caliphate](/wiki/Umayyad_Caliphate "Umayyad Caliphate") and its replacement by the [Abbasid Caliphate](/wiki/Abbasid_Caliphate "Abbasid Caliphate"). The Abbasids moved their capital to [Baghdad](/wiki/Baghdad "Baghdad") and were more concerned with the Middle East than Europe, losing control of sections of the Muslim lands. Umayyad descendants took over the Iberian Peninsula, the [Aghlabids](/wiki/Aghlabids "Aghlabids") controlled North Africa, and the [Tulunids](/wiki/Tulunids "Tulunids") became rulers of Egypt.[[86]](#cite_note-Brown15-94) By the middle of the 8th century, new trading patterns were emerging in the Mediterranean; trade between the Franks and the Arabs replaced the old [Roman economy](/wiki/Roman_economy "Roman economy"). Franks traded timber, furs, swords, and enslaved people in return for silks and other fabrics, spices, and precious metals from the Arabs.[[87]](#cite_note-Cunliffe427-95)

### Trade and economy

Main article: [Medieval economic history](/wiki/Medieval_economic_history "Medieval economic history")

The migrations and invasions of the 4th and 5th centuries disrupted trade networks around the Mediterranean. African goods stopped being imported into Europe, first disappearing from the interior and, by the 7th century, found only in a few cities such as Rome or [Naples](/wiki/Naples "Naples"). By the end of the 7th century, under the impact of the [Muslim conquests](/wiki/Early_Muslim_conquests "Early Muslim conquests"), African products were no longer found in Western Europe. Replacing goods from long-range trade with local products was a trend throughout the old Roman lands in the Early Middle Ages. This was especially marked in the lands that did not lie on the Mediterranean, such as northern Gaul or Britain. Non-local goods appearing in the archaeological record are usually luxury goods. In northern Europe, not only were the trade networks local, but the goods carried were simple, with little pottery or other complex products. Around the Mediterranean, pottery remained prevalent and appears to have been traded over medium-range networks, not just produced locally.[[88]](#cite_note-Wickham218-96)

The various Germanic states in the west all had [coinages](/wiki/Coin "Coin") that imitated existing Roman and Byzantine forms. Gold continued to be minted until the end of the 7th century in 693–694, when it was replaced by silver in the Merovingian kingdom. The basic Frankish silver coin was the [denarius](/wiki/Denarius "Denarius") or [denier](/wiki/French_denier "French denier"), while the Anglo-Saxon version was called a [penny](/wiki/Penny "Penny"). From these areas, the denier or penny spread throughout Europe from 700 to 1000. Copper or bronze coins were not struck, nor were gold, except in Southern Europe. No silver coins denominated in multiple units were minted.[[89]](#cite_note-Coin-97)

### Church and monasticism

Main article: [Christianity in the Middle Ages](/wiki/Christianity_in_the_Middle_Ages "Christianity in the Middle Ages")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/03/Gregory_I_-_Antiphonary_of_Hartker_of_Sankt_Gallen.jpg/250px-Gregory_I_-_Antiphonary_of_Hartker_of_Sankt_Gallen.jpg)](/wiki/File:Gregory_I_-_Antiphonary_of_Hartker_of_Sankt_Gallen.jpg)

11th-century miniature of [Gregory the Great](/wiki/Gregory_the_Great "Gregory the Great") dictating to a secretary

Christianity was a major unifying factor between Eastern and Western Europe before the Arab conquests, but the conquest of North Africa sundered maritime connections between those areas. Increasingly, the Byzantine Church differed in language, practices, and [liturgy](/wiki/Liturgy "Liturgy") from the Western Church. The Eastern Church used Greek instead of Western Latin. Theological and political differences emerged, and by the early and middle 8th century, issues such as [iconoclasm](/wiki/Byzantine_Iconoclasm "Byzantine Iconoclasm"), [clerical marriage](/wiki/Clerical_marriage "Clerical marriage"), and [state control of the Church](/wiki/Caesaropapism "Caesaropapism") had widened to the extent that the cultural and religious differences were more significant than the similarities.[[90]](#cite_note-Collins218-98) A formal break known as the [East–West Schism](/wiki/East%E2%80%93West_Schism "East–West Schism") came in 1054, when the [papacy](/wiki/Papacy "Papacy") and the [patriarchy of Constantinople](/wiki/Ecumenical_Patriarch_of_Constantinople "Ecumenical Patriarch of Constantinople") clashed over [papal supremacy](/wiki/Papal_supremacy "Papal supremacy") and [excommunicated](/wiki/Excommunicated "Excommunicated") each other, which led to the division of Christianity into two Churches—the Western branch became the [Roman Catholic Church](/wiki/Roman_Catholic_Church "Roman Catholic Church") and the Eastern branch the [Eastern Orthodox Church](/wiki/Eastern_Orthodox_Church "Eastern Orthodox Church").[[91]](#cite_note-Davies328-99)

The [ecclesiastical structure](/wiki/Hierarchy_of_the_Catholic_Church "Hierarchy of the Catholic Church") of the Roman Empire survived the movements and invasions in the West mostly intact. Still, the papacy was little regarded, and few of the Western [bishops](/wiki/Bishop "Bishop") looked to the bishop of Rome for religious or political leadership. [Many of the popes](/wiki/Byzantine_Papacy "Byzantine Papacy") before 750 were more concerned with Byzantine affairs and Eastern theological controversies. The register, or archived copies of the letters, of Pope [Gregory the Great](/wiki/Gregory_the_Great "Gregory the Great") (pope 590–604) survived. Of those 850 letters, most were concerned with affairs in Italy or Constantinople. The only part of Western Europe where the papacy had influence was Britain, where Gregory had sent the [Gregorian mission](/wiki/Gregorian_mission "Gregorian mission") in 597 to convert the Anglo-Saxons to Christianity.[[92]](#cite_note-WIckham170-100) [Irish missionaries](/wiki/Irish_missionaries "Irish missionaries") were most active in Western Europe between the 5th and the 7th centuries, going first to England and Scotland and then on to the continent. Under such [monks](/wiki/Monk "Monk") as [Columba](/wiki/Columba "Columba") (d. 597) and [Columbanus](/wiki/Columbanus "Columbanus") (d. 615), they founded monasteries, taught in Latin and Greek, and authored secular and religious works.[[93]](#cite_note-Colish62-101)

The Early Middle Ages witnessed the rise of [monasticism](/wiki/Christian_monasticism "Christian monasticism") in the West. The shape of European monasticism was determined by traditions and ideas that originated with the [Desert Fathers](/wiki/Desert_Fathers "Desert Fathers") of [Egypt](/wiki/Egypt "Egypt") and [Syria](/wiki/Syria "Syria"). Most European monasteries were of the type that focuses on the community experience of the spiritual life, called [cenobitism](/wiki/Cenobitism "Cenobitism"), which was pioneered by [Pachomius](/wiki/Pachomius "Pachomius") (d. 348) in the 4th century. Monastic ideals spread from Egypt to Western Europe in the 5th and 6th centuries through [hagiographical literature](/wiki/Hagiography "Hagiography") such as the *[Life of Anthony](/wiki/Life_of_Anthony "Life of Anthony")*.[[94]](#cite_note-Lawrence10-102) [Benedict of Nursia](/wiki/Benedict_of_Nursia "Benedict of Nursia") (d. 547) wrote the [Benedictine Rule](/wiki/Benedictine_Rule "Benedictine Rule") for Western monasticism during the 6th century, detailing the administrative and spiritual responsibilities of a community of monks led by an [abbot](/wiki/Abbot "Abbot").[[95]](#cite_note-Lawrence18-103) Monks and monasteries had a profound effect on the religious and political life of the Early Middle Ages, in various cases acting as [land trusts](/wiki/Land_trust "Land trust") for powerful families, centres of propaganda and royal support in newly conquered regions, and bases for missions and proselytisation.[[96]](#cite_note-Wickham185-104) They were the main and sometimes only outposts of education and literacy in a region. Many of the surviving manuscripts of the Latin [classics](/wiki/Classics#Classical_Rome "Classics") were copied in monasteries in the Early Middle Ages.[[97]](#cite_note-Hamilton43-105) Monks were also the authors of new works, including history, theology, and other subjects, written by authors such as [Bede](/wiki/Bede "Bede") (d. 735), a native of northern England who wrote in the late 7th and early 8th centuries.[[98]](#cite_note-Colish64-106)

### Carolingian Europe

Main articles: [Francia](/wiki/Francia "Francia") and [Carolingian Empire](/wiki/Carolingian_Empire "Carolingian Empire")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/16/Frankish_Empire_481_to_814-en.svg/500px-Frankish_Empire_481_to_814-en.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Frankish_Empire_481_to_814-en.svg)

Map showing growth of Frankish power from 481 to 814

The Frankish kingdom in northern Gaul split into kingdoms called [Austrasia](/wiki/Austrasia "Austrasia"), [Neustria](/wiki/Neustria "Neustria"), and [Burgundy](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Burgundy "Kingdom of Burgundy") during the 6th and 7th centuries, all of them ruled by the Merovingian dynasty, who were descended from Clovis. The 7th century was a tumultuous period of wars between Austrasia and Neustria.[[99]](#cite_note-Bauer246-107) Such warfare was exploited by [Pippin I](/wiki/Pippin_of_Landen "Pippin of Landen") (d. 640), the [Mayor of the Palace](/wiki/Mayor_of_the_Palace "Mayor of the Palace") for Austrasia who became the power behind the Austrasian throne. Later, his family inherited the office and acted as advisers and regents. One of his descendants, [Charles Martel](/wiki/Charles_Martel "Charles Martel") (d. 741), won the [Battle of Poitiers](/wiki/Battle_of_Poitiers_(732) "Battle of Poitiers (732)") in 732, halting the advance of Muslim armies across the Pyrenees.[[100]](#cite_note-Bauer347-108)[[I]](#cite_note-110) Great Britain was divided into small states dominated by the kingdoms of [Northumbria](/wiki/Northumbria "Northumbria"), [Mercia](/wiki/Mercia "Mercia"), [Wessex](/wiki/Wessex "Wessex"), and [East Anglia](/wiki/East_Anglia "East Anglia") which descended from the Anglo-Saxon invaders. Smaller kingdoms in present-day Wales and Scotland were still under the control of the native Britons and [Picts](/wiki/Picts "Picts").[[102]](#cite_note-Wickham158-111) Ireland was divided into even smaller political units, usually known as tribal kingdoms, under the control of kings. There were perhaps as many as [150 local kings](/wiki/List_of_Irish_kingdoms "List of Irish kingdoms") in Ireland of varying importance.[[103]](#cite_note-Wickham164-112)

The [Carolingian dynasty](/wiki/Carolingian_dynasty "Carolingian dynasty"), as the successors to Charles Martel are known, officially took control of the kingdoms of Austrasia and Neustria in a coup of 753 led by [Pippin III](/wiki/Pippin_III "Pippin III") (r. 752–768). A contemporary chronicle claims that Pippin sought and gained authority for this coup from Pope [Stephen II](/wiki/Pope_Stephen_II "Pope Stephen II") (pope 752–757). Pippin's takeover was reinforced with propaganda that portrayed the Merovingians as inept or cruel rulers, exalted the accomplishments of Charles Martel, and circulated stories of the family's great piety. At the time of his death in 768, Pippin left his kingdom in the hands of his two sons, Charles (r. 768–814) and [Carloman](/wiki/Carloman_I "Carloman I") (r. 768–771). When Carloman died of natural causes, Charles blocked the succession of Carloman's young son and installed himself as the king of the united Austrasia and Neustria. Charles, more often known as Charles the Great or [Charlemagne](/wiki/Charlemagne "Charlemagne"), embarked upon a programme of systematic expansion in 774 that unified a large portion of Europe, eventually controlling modern-day France, northern Italy, and [Saxony](/wiki/Saxon_Wars "Saxon Wars"). In the wars that lasted beyond 800, he rewarded allies with war booty and command over parcels of land.[[104]](#cite_note-Bauer371-113) In 774, Charlemagne conquered the Lombards, which freed the papacy from the fear of Lombard conquest and marked the beginnings of the [Papal States](/wiki/Papal_States "Papal States").[[105]](#cite_note-Brown20-114)[[J]](#cite_note-116)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4d/Aachener_Dom_Pfalzkapelle_vom_M%C3%BCnsterplatz_2014.jpg/250px-Aachener_Dom_Pfalzkapelle_vom_M%C3%BCnsterplatz_2014.jpg)](/wiki/File:Aachener_Dom_Pfalzkapelle_vom_M%C3%BCnsterplatz_2014.jpg)

[Charlemagne's palace chapel](/wiki/Palatine_Chapel,_Aachen "Palatine Chapel, Aachen") at [Aachen](/wiki/Aachen "Aachen"), completed in 805[[107]](#cite_note-Stalley73-117)

The coronation of Charlemagne as emperor on Christmas Day 800 is regarded as a turning point in medieval history, marking a return of the Western Roman Empire since the new emperor ruled over much of the area previously controlled by the Western emperors.[[108]](#cite_note-Backman109-118) It also marks a change in Charlemagne's relationship with the Byzantine Empire, as the assumption of the imperial title by the Carolingians asserted their equivalence to the Byzantine state.[[109]](#cite_note-Backman117-119) There were several differences between the newly established Carolingian Empire and both the older Western Roman Empire and the concurrent Byzantine Empire. The Frankish lands were rural, with only a few small cities. Most of the people were peasants who settled on small farms. Little trade existed, and much of that was with the British Isles and Scandinavia, in contrast to the older Roman Empire with its trading networks centred on the Mediterranean.[[108]](#cite_note-Backman109-118) The empire was administered by an itinerant court that travelled with the emperor, as well as approximately 300 imperial officials called [counts](/wiki/Count "Count"), who administered the [counties](/wiki/Counties "Counties") the empire had been divided into. Clergy and local bishops served as officials, as well as the imperial officials, called *[missi dominici](/wiki/Missi_dominici "Missi dominici")*, who served as roving inspectors and troubleshooters.[[110]](#cite_note-Davies302-120)

### Carolingian Renaissance

Main article: [Carolingian Renaissance](/wiki/Carolingian_Renaissance "Carolingian Renaissance")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9b/Kloster_Lorsch_07.jpg/330px-Kloster_Lorsch_07.jpg)](/wiki/File:Kloster_Lorsch_07.jpg)

[Lorsch Abbey](/wiki/Lorsch_Abbey "Lorsch Abbey") gatehouse, c. 800, an example of [Carolingian architecture](/wiki/Carolingian_architecture "Carolingian architecture")—a first, albeit isolated [classical architecture](/wiki/Classical_architecture "Classical architecture") movement

Charlemagne's court in [Aachen](/wiki/Aachen "Aachen") was the centre of the cultural revival, sometimes referred to as the "[Carolingian Renaissance](/wiki/Carolingian_Renaissance "Carolingian Renaissance")". Literacy increased, as did development in the arts, architecture, jurisprudence, and liturgical and scriptural studies. The English monk [Alcuin](/wiki/Alcuin "Alcuin") (d. 804) was invited to Aachen and brought the [education](/wiki/Northumbria%27s_Golden_Age "Northumbria's Golden Age") available in the monasteries of Northumbria. Charlemagne's [chancery](/wiki/Chancery_(medieval_office) "Chancery (medieval office)")—or writing office—made use of a new [script](/wiki/Writing_system "Writing system") today known as [Carolingian minuscule](/wiki/Carolingian_minuscule "Carolingian minuscule"),[[K]](#cite_note-122) allowing a standard writing style that advanced communication across much of Europe. Charlemagne sponsored changes in [church liturgy](/wiki/Christian_liturgy "Christian liturgy"), imposing the Roman form of church service on his domains, as well as the [Gregorian chant](/wiki/Gregorian_chant "Gregorian chant") in liturgical music for the churches. An important activity for scholars during this period was copying, correcting, and disseminating basic works on religious and secular topics to encourage learning. New works on religious topics and schoolbooks were also produced.[[112]](#cite_note-Colish66-123) Grammarians of the period modified the Latin language, changing it from the [Classical Latin](/wiki/Classical_Latin "Classical Latin") of the Roman Empire into a more flexible form to fit the needs of the Church and government. By the reign of Charlemagne, the language had so diverged from the classical Latin that it was later called [Medieval Latin](/wiki/Medieval_Latin "Medieval Latin").[[113]](#cite_note-Lang-124)

### Breakup of the Carolingian Empire

Main articles: [Holy Roman Empire](/wiki/Holy_Roman_Empire "Holy Roman Empire") and [Viking Age](/wiki/Viking_Age "Viking Age")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/13/Carolingian_territorial_divisions%2C_843.png/250px-Carolingian_territorial_divisions%2C_843.png)](/wiki/File:Carolingian_territorial_divisions,_843.png)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b0/Carolingian_territorial_divisions%2C_855.png/250px-Carolingian_territorial_divisions%2C_855.png)](/wiki/File:Carolingian_territorial_divisions,_855.png)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/30/Carolingian_territorial_divisions%2C_870.png/250px-Carolingian_territorial_divisions%2C_870.png)](/wiki/File:Carolingian_territorial_divisions,_870.png)

Territorial divisions of the [Carolingian Empire](/wiki/Carolingian_Empire "Carolingian Empire") in 843, 855, and 870

Charlemagne planned to continue the Frankish tradition of dividing his kingdom between all his heirs but was unable to do so as only one son, [Louis the Pious](/wiki/Louis_the_Pious "Louis the Pious") (r. 814–840), was still alive by 813. Just before Charlemagne died in 814, he crowned Louis as his successor. Numerous divisions of the empire marked Louis's reign of 26 years among his sons and, after 829, civil wars between various alliances of father and sons over the control of various parts of the empire. Eventually, Louis recognised his eldest son [Lothair I](/wiki/Lothair_I "Lothair I") (d. 855) as emperor and gave him Italy.[[L]](#cite_note-126) Louis divided the rest of the empire between Lothair and [Charles the Bald](/wiki/Charles_the_Bald "Charles the Bald") (d. 877), his youngest son. Lothair took [East Francia](/wiki/East_Francia "East Francia"), comprising both banks of the Rhine and eastwards, leaving Charles [West Francia](/wiki/West_Francia "West Francia") with the empire to the west of the Rhineland and the Alps. [Louis the German](/wiki/Louis_the_German "Louis the German") (d. 876), the middle child, who had been rebellious to the last, was allowed to keep Bavaria under the [suzerainty](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/suzerainty "wikt:suzerainty") of his elder brother. The division was disputed. [Pepin II of Aquitaine](/wiki/Pepin_II_of_Aquitaine "Pepin II of Aquitaine") (d. after 864), the emperor's grandson, rebelled in a contest for [Aquitaine](/wiki/Aquitaine "Aquitaine"), while Louis the German tried to annexe all of East Francia. Louis the Pious died in 840, with the empire still in chaos.[[115]](#cite_note-Bauer427-127)

A three-year civil war followed his death. By the [Treaty of Verdun](/wiki/Treaty_of_Verdun "Treaty of Verdun") (843), a kingdom between the [Rhine](/wiki/Rhine "Rhine") and [Rhone](/wiki/Rhone "Rhone") rivers was created for Lothair to go with his lands in Italy, and his imperial title was recognised. Louis the German controlled Bavaria and the eastern lands in modern-day Germany. Charles the Bald received the western Frankish lands, comprising most of modern-day France.[[115]](#cite_note-Bauer427-127) Charlemagne's grandsons and great-grandsons divided their kingdoms between their descendants, eventually causing all internal cohesion to be lost.[[116]](#cite_note-Backman139-128)[[M]](#cite_note-130) In 987, the Carolingian dynasty was replaced in the western lands, with the crowning of [Hugh Capet](/wiki/Hugh_Capet "Hugh Capet") (r. 987–996) as king.[[N]](#cite_note-133)[[O]](#cite_note-134) In the eastern lands, the dynasty had died out earlier, in 911, with the death of [Louis the Child](/wiki/Louis_the_Child "Louis the Child"),[[119]](#cite_note-Collins360-132) and the selection of the unrelated [Conrad I](/wiki/Conrad_I_of_Germany "Conrad I of Germany") (r. 911–918) as king.[[120]](#cite_note-Collins397-135)

Invasions, migrations, and raids by external foes accompanied the break-up of the Carolingian Empire. The Atlantic and northern shores were harassed by the [Vikings](/wiki/Vikings "Vikings"), who also raided the British Isles and settled there and in Iceland. In 911, the Viking chieftain [Rollo](/wiki/Rollo "Rollo") (d. c. 931) received permission from the Frankish King [Charles the Simple](/wiki/Charles_the_Simple "Charles the Simple") (r. 898–922) to settle in what became [Normandy](/wiki/Normandy "Normandy").[[121]](#cite_note-Backman141-136)[[P]](#cite_note-138) The eastern parts of the Frankish kingdoms, especially Germany and Italy, were under continual [Magyar](/wiki/Hungarian_people "Hungarian people") assault until the invader's defeat at the [Battle of Lechfeld](/wiki/Battle_of_Lechfeld "Battle of Lechfeld") in 955.[[123]](#cite_note-Backman144-139) The break-up of the Abbasid dynasty meant that the Islamic world fragmented into smaller political states, some of which began expanding into Italy and Sicily, as well as over the Pyrenees into the southern parts of the Frankish kingdoms.[[124]](#cite_note-Bauer147-140)

### New kingdoms and Byzantine revival

Main articles: [Byzantine Empire under the Macedonian dynasty](/wiki/Byzantine_Empire_under_the_Macedonian_dynasty "Byzantine Empire under the Macedonian dynasty"), [Byzantine Empire under the Isaurian dynasty](/wiki/Byzantine_Empire_under_the_Isaurian_dynasty "Byzantine Empire under the Isaurian dynasty"), [First Bulgarian Empire](/wiki/First_Bulgarian_Empire "First Bulgarian Empire"), [Christianisation of Bulgaria](/wiki/Christianisation_of_Bulgaria "Christianisation of Bulgaria"), [Kingdom of Germany](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Germany "Kingdom of Germany"), [Christianisation of Scandinavia](/wiki/Christianisation_of_Scandinavia "Christianisation of Scandinavia"), and [Christianisation of Kievan Rus'](/wiki/Christianisation_of_Kievan_Rus%27 "Christianisation of Kievan Rus'")

See also: [Byzantine–Arab wars (780–1180)](/wiki/Byzantine%E2%80%93Arab_wars_(780%E2%80%931180) "Byzantine–Arab wars (780–1180)") and [Byzantine–Bulgarian wars](/wiki/Byzantine%E2%80%93Bulgarian_wars "Byzantine–Bulgarian wars")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/39/Europe_in_900_AD.png/330px-Europe_in_900_AD.png)](/wiki/File:Europe_in_900_AD.png)

Europe in 900

Efforts by local kings to fight the invaders led to the formation of new political entities. In [Anglo-Saxon England](/wiki/Anglo-Saxon_England "Anglo-Saxon England"), King [Alfred the Great](/wiki/Alfred_the_Great "Alfred the Great") (r. 871–899) came to an agreement with the Viking invaders in the late 9th century, resulting in [Danish settlements](/wiki/Danelaw "Danelaw") in Northumbria, Mercia, and parts of East Anglia.[[125]](#cite_note-Collins378-141) By the middle of the 10th century, Alfred's successors had conquered Northumbria and restored English control over most of the southern part of Great Britain.[[126]](#cite_note-Collins387-142) In northern Britain, [Kenneth MacAlpin](/wiki/Kenneth_MacAlpin "Kenneth MacAlpin") (d. c. 860) united the Picts and the [Scots](/wiki/Scottish_people "Scottish people") into the [Kingdom of Alba](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Alba "Kingdom of Alba").[[127]](#cite_note-Davies309-143) In the early 10th century, the [Ottonian dynasty](/wiki/Ottonian_dynasty "Ottonian dynasty") had established itself in [Germany](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Germany "Kingdom of Germany"), and was engaged in driving back the Magyars. Its efforts culminated in the coronation in 962 of [Otto I](/wiki/Otto_I "Otto I") (r. 936–973) as [Holy Roman Emperor](/wiki/Holy_Roman_Emperor "Holy Roman Emperor").[[128]](#cite_note-Collins394-144) In 972, he secured recognition of his title by the Byzantine Empire, which he sealed with the marriage of his son [Otto II](/wiki/Otto_II "Otto II") (r. 967–983) to [Theophanu](/wiki/Theophanu "Theophanu") (d. 991), daughter of an earlier Byzantine Emperor [Romanos II](/wiki/Romanos_II "Romanos II") (r. 959–963).[[129]](#cite_note-Davies317-145) By the late 10th century [Italy](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Italy_(medieval) "Kingdom of Italy (medieval)") had been drawn into the Ottonian sphere after a period of instability;[[130]](#cite_note-Wickham435-146) [Otto III](/wiki/Otto_III "Otto III") (r. 996–1002) spent much of his later reign in the kingdom.[[131]](#cite_note-Whitton152-147) The western Frankish kingdom was more fragmented, and although kings remained nominally in charge, much of the political power devolved to the local lords.[[132]](#cite_note-Wickham439-148)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d8/Christ_Magdeburg_Cathedral_Met_41.100.157.jpg/250px-Christ_Magdeburg_Cathedral_Met_41.100.157.jpg)](/wiki/File:Christ_Magdeburg_Cathedral_Met_41.100.157.jpg)

10th-century [Ottonian](/wiki/Ottonian_art "Ottonian art") ivory plaque from the [Magdeburg Ivories](/wiki/Magdeburg_Ivories "Magdeburg Ivories"); Christ receiving a church from [Otto I](/wiki/Otto_I "Otto I")

[Missionary efforts to Scandinavia](/wiki/Christianization_of_Scandinavia "Christianization of Scandinavia") during the 9th and 10th centuries helped strengthen the growth of kingdoms such as [Sweden](/wiki/History_of_Sweden_(800%E2%80%931521) "History of Sweden (800–1521)"), [Denmark](/wiki/History_of_Denmark "History of Denmark"), and [Norway](/wiki/History_of_Norway "History of Norway"), which gained power and territory. Some kings converted to Christianity, although not all by 1000. Scandinavians also expanded and colonised throughout Europe. Besides the settlements in Ireland, England, and Normandy, further settlement took place in what became [Russia](/wiki/Kyivan_Rus%27 "Kyivan Rus'") and [Iceland](/wiki/Settlement_of_Iceland "Settlement of Iceland"). Swedish traders and raiders ranged down the rivers of the Russian steppe and even attempted to seize Constantinople in [860](/wiki/Rus%27%E2%80%93Byzantine_War_(860) "Rus'–Byzantine War (860)") and [907](/wiki/Rus%27%E2%80%93Byzantine_War_(907) "Rus'–Byzantine War (907)").[[133]](#cite_note-Collins385-149) Christian Spain, initially driven into a small section of the peninsula in the north, expanded slowly south during the 9th and 10th centuries, establishing the kingdoms of [Asturias](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Asturias "Kingdom of Asturias") and [León](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Le%C3%B3n "Kingdom of León").[[134]](#cite_note-Wickham500-150)

In Eastern Europe, Byzantium revived its fortunes under Emperor [Basil I](/wiki/Basil_I "Basil I") (r. 867–886) and his successors [Leo VI](/wiki/Leo_VI_the_Wise "Leo VI the Wise") (r. 886–912) and [Constantine VII](/wiki/Constantine_VII "Constantine VII") (r. 913–959), members of the [Macedonian dynasty](/wiki/Macedonian_dynasty "Macedonian dynasty"). Commerce revived, and the emperors oversaw the extension of a uniform administration to all the provinces. The military was reorganised, which allowed the emperors [John I](/wiki/John_I_Tzimiskes "John I Tzimiskes") (r. 969–976) and [Basil II](/wiki/Basil_II "Basil II") (r. 976–1025) to expand the frontiers of the empire on all fronts. The imperial court was the centre of a revival of classical learning, a process known as the [Macedonian Renaissance](/wiki/Macedonian_Renaissance "Macedonian Renaissance"). Writers such as [John Geometres](/wiki/John_Geometres "John Geometres") ([fl.](/wiki/Fl. "Fl.") early 10th century) composed new hymns, poems, and other works.[[135]](#cite_note-Davies318-151) Missionary efforts by both Eastern and Western clergy resulted in the conversion of the [Moravians](/wiki/Moravians "Moravians"), [Bulgars](/wiki/Bulgars "Bulgars"), [Bohemians](/wiki/Bohemian_(demonym) "Bohemian (demonym)"), [Poles](/wiki/Poles_(people) "Poles (people)"), Magyars, and Slavic inhabitants of the [Kievan Rus'](/wiki/Kievan_Rus%27 "Kievan Rus'"). These conversions contributed to the founding of political states in the lands of those peoples—the states of [Moravia](/wiki/Great_Moravia "Great Moravia"), [Bulgaria](/wiki/First_Bulgarian_Empire "First Bulgarian Empire"), [Bohemia](/wiki/Duchy_of_Bohemia "Duchy of Bohemia"), [Poland](/wiki/Poland_in_the_Early_Middle_Ages "Poland in the Early Middle Ages"), Hungary, and the Kievan Rus'.[[136]](#cite_note-Davies321-152) Bulgaria, which was founded around 680, at its height reached from Budapest to the Black Sea and from the Dnieper River in modern Ukraine to the Adriatic Sea.[[137]](#cite_note-Crampton12-153) By 1018, the last Bulgarian nobles had surrendered to the Byzantine Empire.[[138]](#cite_note-Curta246-154)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b1/KellsFol032vChristEnthroned.jpg/250px-KellsFol032vChristEnthroned.jpg)](/wiki/File:KellsFol032vChristEnthroned.jpg)

A page from the [Book of Kells](/wiki/Book_of_Kells "Book of Kells"), an [illuminated manuscript](/wiki/Illuminated_manuscript "Illuminated manuscript") created in the British Isles in the late 8th or early 9th century[[139]](#cite_note-Nees145-155)

Few large stone buildings were constructed between the Constantinian [basilicas](/wiki/Basilica "Basilica") of the 4th and 8th centuries, although many smaller ones were built during the 6th and 7th centuries.[*[failed verification](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability")*] By the beginning of the 8th century, the Carolingian Empire revived the basilica form of architecture.[[140]](#cite_note-Stalley29-156) One feature of the basilica is the use of a [transept](/wiki/Transept "Transept"),[[141]](#cite_note-Stalley43-157) or the "arms" of a cross-shaped building that are perpendicular to the long [nave](/wiki/Nave "Nave").[[142]](#cite_note-Cosman247-158) Other new features of religious architecture include the [crossing tower](/wiki/Crossing_tower "Crossing tower") and a monumental [entrance to the church](/wiki/Architecture_of_cathedrals_and_great_churches#Façade "Architecture of cathedrals and great churches"), usually at the west end of the building.[[143]](#cite_note-Stalley49-159)

[Carolingian art](/wiki/Carolingian_art "Carolingian art") was produced for a small group of figures around the court and the monasteries and churches they supported. It was dominated by efforts to regain the dignity and classicism of imperial Roman and [Byzantine art](/wiki/Byzantine_art "Byzantine art") but was also influenced by the [Insular art](/wiki/Insular_art "Insular art") of the British Isles. Insular art integrated the energy of [Irish Celtic](/wiki/Celtic_art "Celtic art") and [Anglo-Saxon Germanic](/wiki/Anglo-Saxon_art "Anglo-Saxon art") styles of ornament with Mediterranean forms such as the book, and established many characteristics of art for the rest of the medieval period. Surviving religious works from the Early Middle Ages are mostly [illuminated manuscripts](/wiki/Illuminated_manuscript "Illuminated manuscript") and carved [ivories](/wiki/Ivory_carving#Antiquity_and_the_Early_Medieval_period "Ivory carving"), originally made for metalwork that has since been melted down.[[144]](#cite_note-Kitzinger36-160)[[145]](#cite_note-Henderson18-161) Objects in precious metals were the most prestigious form of art, but almost all are lost except for a few crosses such as the [Cross of Lothair](/wiki/Cross_of_Lothair "Cross of Lothair"), several [reliquaries](/wiki/Reliquaries "Reliquaries"), and finds such as the Anglo-Saxon burial at [Sutton Hoo](/wiki/Sutton_Hoo "Sutton Hoo") and the [hoards](/wiki/Hoard "Hoard") of [Gourdon](/wiki/Treasure_of_Gourdon "Treasure of Gourdon") from Merovingian France, [Guarrazar](/wiki/Guarrazar "Guarrazar") from Visigothic Spain and [Nagyszentmiklós](/wiki/Treasure_of_Nagyszentmikl%C3%B3s "Treasure of Nagyszentmiklós") near Byzantine territory. There are survivals from the large [brooches](/wiki/Brooch "Brooch") in [fibula](/wiki/Fibula_(brooch) "Fibula (brooch)") or [penannular](/wiki/Celtic_brooch "Celtic brooch") form that were key pieces of personal adornment for elites, including the Irish [Tara Brooch](/wiki/Tara_Brooch "Tara Brooch").[[146]](#cite_note-Henderson36-162)[*[failed verification](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability")*] Highly decorated books were mostly [Gospel Books](/wiki/Gospel_Book "Gospel Book") and these have survived in [larger numbers](/wiki/List_of_illuminated_manuscripts "List of illuminated manuscripts"), including the Insular [Book of Kells](/wiki/Book_of_Kells "Book of Kells"), the [Book of Lindisfarne](/wiki/Book_of_Lindisfarne "Book of Lindisfarne"), and the imperial [Codex Aureus of St. Emmeram](/wiki/Codex_Aureus_of_St._Emmeram "Codex Aureus of St. Emmeram"), which is one of the few to retain its "[treasure binding](/wiki/Treasure_binding "Treasure binding")" of gold encrusted with jewels.[[147]](#cite_note-Benton41-163) Charlemagne's court seems to have been responsible for the acceptance of figurative [monumental sculpture](/wiki/Monumental_sculpture "Monumental sculpture") in [Christian art](/wiki/Christian_art "Christian art"),[[148]](#cite_note-Lasko16-164) and by the end of the period near life-sized figures such as the [Gero Cross](/wiki/Gero_Cross "Gero Cross") were common in important churches.[[149]](#cite_note-Henderson233-165)

### Military and technological developments

During the later Roman Empire, the principal military developments were attempts to create an effective cavalry force and the continued development of highly specialised types of troops. The creation of heavily armoured [cataphract](/wiki/Cataphract "Cataphract")-type soldiers as cavalry was an important feature of the 5th-century Roman military. The various invading tribes had differing emphases on types of soldiers—ranging from the primarily infantry Anglo-Saxon invaders of Britain to the Vandals and Visigoths, who had a high proportion of cavalry in their armies.[[150]](#cite_note-NicolleWestern28-166) During the early invasion period, the [stirrup](/wiki/Stirrup "Stirrup") had not been introduced into warfare, which limited the usefulness of cavalry as [shock troops](/wiki/Shock_troops "Shock troops") because it was not possible to put the full force of the horse and rider behind blows struck by the rider.[[151]](#cite_note-NicolleWestern30-167) The greatest change in military affairs during the invasion period was the adoption of the Hunnic [composite bow](/wiki/Composite_bow "Composite bow") in place of the earlier, and weaker, [Scythian](/wiki/Scythia "Scythia") composite bow.[[152]](#cite_note-NicolleWestern31-168) Another development was the increasing use of [longswords](/wiki/Longsword "Longsword")[[153]](#cite_note-NicolleWestern34-169) and the progressive replacement of [scale armour](/wiki/Scale_armour "Scale armour") by [mail armour](/wiki/Mail_armour "Mail armour") and [lamellar armour](/wiki/Lamellar_armour "Lamellar armour").[[154]](#cite_note-NicolleWestern39-170)

The importance of infantry and light cavalry declined during the early Carolingian period, with a growing dominance of elite heavy cavalry. The use of [militia-type levies](/wiki/Conscription "Conscription") of the free population declined over the Carolingian period.[[155]](#cite_note-NicolleWestern58-171) Although much of the Carolingian armies were mounted, a large proportion during the early period appear to have been [mounted infantry](/wiki/Mounted_infantry "Mounted infantry"), rather than true cavalry.[[156]](#cite_note-NicolleWestern76-172) One exception was Anglo-Saxon England, where the armies were still composed of regional levies, known as the *[fyrd](/wiki/Fyrd "Fyrd")*, which were led by the local elites.[[157]](#cite_note-NicolleWestern67-173) In military technology, one of the main changes was the return of the [crossbow](/wiki/Crossbow "Crossbow"), which had been known in Roman times and reappeared as a military weapon during the last part of the Early Middle Ages.[[158]](#cite_note-NicolleWestern80-174) Another change was the introduction of the stirrup, which increased the effectiveness of cavalry as shock troops. A technological advance that had implications beyond the military was the [horseshoe](/wiki/Horseshoe "Horseshoe"), which allowed horses to be used in rocky terrain.[[159]](#cite_note-NicolleWestern88-175)

## High Middle Ages

Main article: [High Middle Ages](/wiki/High_Middle_Ages "High Middle Ages")

### Society and economic life
