Further information: [Agriculture in the Middle Ages](/wiki/Agriculture_in_the_Middle_Ages "Agriculture in the Middle Ages")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/31/Cleric%2C_Knight%2C_and_Workman_representing_the_Three_Classes.jpg/250px-Cleric%2C_Knight%2C_and_Workman_representing_the_Three_Classes.jpg)](/wiki/File:Cleric,_Knight,_and_Workman_representing_the_Three_Classes.jpg)

Medieval French [manuscript illustration](/wiki/Illuminated_manuscript "Illuminated manuscript") of the three [classes](/wiki/Social_class "Social class") of medieval society: those who prayed (the [clergy](/wiki/Clergy "Clergy")) those who fought (the [knights](/wiki/Knight "Knight")), and those who worked (the [peasantry](/wiki/Peasant "Peasant")).[[160]](#cite_note-Whitton134-176) The relationship between these classes was governed by [feudalism](/wiki/Feudalism "Feudalism") and [manorialism](/wiki/Manorialism "Manorialism").[[161]](#cite_note-Gainty352-177) (*Li Livres dou Sante*, 13th century)

##### Peasantry

The High Middle Ages was a period of tremendous [population expansion](/wiki/Medieval_demography "Medieval demography"). The estimated population of Europe grew from 35 to 80 million between 1000 and 1347, although the exact causes remain unclear: improved agricultural techniques, the decline of slaveholding, a [more clement climate](/wiki/Medieval_Warm_Period "Medieval Warm Period") and the lack of invasion have all been suggested.[[162]](#cite_note-Jordan5-178)[[163]](#cite_note-Backman156-179) As much as 90 per cent of the European population remained rural peasants. Many were no longer settled in isolated farms but had gathered into small communities, usually known as [manors](/wiki/Manorialism "Manorialism") or villages.[[163]](#cite_note-Backman156-179) These peasants were often subject to noble overlords and owed them rents and other services in a system known as [manorialism](/wiki/Manorialism "Manorialism"). There remained a few free peasants throughout this period and beyond,[[164]](#cite_note-Backman164-180) with more of them in the regions of Southern Europe than in the north. The practice of [assarting](/wiki/Assarting "Assarting"), or bringing new lands into production by offering incentives to the peasants who settled them, also contributed to population expansion.[[165]](#cite_note-Epstein52-181)

The [open-field system](/wiki/Open-field_system "Open-field system") of agriculture was commonly practised in most of Europe, especially in "northwestern and central Europe".[[166]](#cite_note-Pounds166-182) Such agricultural communities had three essential characteristics: individual peasant holdings in the form of strips of land were scattered among the different fields belonging to the manor; crops were rotated from year to year to preserve soil fertility; and common land was used for grazing livestock and other purposes. Some regions used a three-field system of crop rotation; others retained the older two-field system.[[167]](#cite_note-Agri-183)

##### Nobility, clergy, and townspeople

Other sections of society included the nobility, clergy, and townspeople. Nobles, both the titled [nobility](/wiki/Nobility "Nobility") and simple [knights](/wiki/Knight "Knight"), exploited the manors and the peasants. However, they did not own lands outright but were granted rights to the income from a manor or other lands by an overlord through the system of [feudalism](/wiki/Feudalism "Feudalism"). During the 11th and 12th centuries, these lands, or [fiefs](/wiki/Fief "Fief"), came to be considered hereditary. In most areas, they were no longer divisible between all the heirs, as had been the case in the early medieval period. Instead, most fiefs and lands went to the eldest son.[[168]](#cite_note-Barber37-184)[[Q]](#cite_note-186) The dominance of the nobility was built upon its control of the land, its military service as [heavy cavalry](/wiki/Heavy_cavalry "Heavy cavalry"), control of [castles](/wiki/Castle "Castle"), and various immunities from taxes or other impositions.[[R]](#cite_note-188) Castles, initially in wood but later in stone, began to be constructed in the 9th and 10th centuries in response to the disorder of the time, and protected from invaders and allowing lords defence from rivals. Control of castles allowed the nobles to defy kings or other overlords.[[170]](#cite_note-Davies311-187) Nobles were stratified; kings and the highest-ranking nobility controlled large numbers of commoners and large tracts of land, as well as other nobles. Beneath them, lesser nobles had authority over smaller land areas and fewer people. Knights were the lowest level of nobility; they controlled but did not own land and had to serve other nobles.[[171]](#cite_note-Daily3-189)[[S]](#cite_note-191)

The clergy was divided into two types: the [secular clergy](/wiki/Secular_clergy "Secular clergy"), who lived out in the world, and the [regular clergy](/wiki/Regular_clergy "Regular clergy"), who lived isolated under a religious rule and usually consisted of monks.[[173]](#cite_note-Hamilton33-192) Throughout the period, monks remained a tiny proportion of the population, usually less than one percent.[[174]](#cite_note-Daily143-193) Most of the regular clergy were drawn from the nobility, the same social class that served as the recruiting ground for the upper levels of the secular clergy. The local [parish](/wiki/Parish "Parish") priests were often drawn from the peasant class.[[175]](#cite_note-Barber33-194) Townspeople were somewhat unusual, as they did not fit into the traditional three-fold division of society into nobles, clergy, and peasants. During the 12th and 13th centuries, the ranks of the townspeople expanded greatly as existing towns grew and new population centres were founded.[[176]](#cite_note-Barber48-195) But throughout the Middle Ages, the population of the towns probably never exceeded 10 percent of the total population.[[177]](#cite_note-Daily171-196)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/84/Petrus_alphonsi_dialogues.jpg/250px-Petrus_alphonsi_dialogues.jpg)](/wiki/File:Petrus_alphonsi_dialogues.jpg)

13th-century illustration of a Jew (in pointed [Jewish hat](/wiki/Jewish_hat "Jewish hat")) and the Christian [Petrus Alphonsi](/wiki/Petrus_Alphonsi "Petrus Alphonsi") debating

##### Religious groups

Jews also [spread across Europe](/wiki/Jews_in_the_Middle_Ages "Jews in the Middle Ages") during the period. Communities were established in [Germany](/wiki/History_of_the_Jews_in_Germany "History of the Jews in Germany") and [England](/wiki/History_of_the_Jews_in_England_(1066%E2%80%931290) "History of the Jews in England (1066–1290)") in the 11th and 12th centuries, but [Spanish Jews](/wiki/History_of_the_Jews_in_Spain#Toleration_and_Jewish_immigration_(1085–1212) "History of the Jews in Spain"), long settled in Spain under the Muslims, came under Christian rule and increasing pressure to convert to Christianity.[[80]](#cite_note-Jews191-87) Most Jews were confined to the cities, as they were not allowed to own land or be peasants.[[178]](#cite_note-Epstein54-197)[[T]](#cite_note-198) Besides the Jews, there were other non-Christians on the edges of Europe—pagan Slavs in Eastern Europe and Muslims in Southern Europe.[[179]](#cite_note-Daily13-199)

##### Women

[Women in the Middle Ages](/wiki/Women_in_the_Middle_Ages "Women in the Middle Ages") were officially required to be subordinate to some male, whether their father, husband, or other kinsman. Widows were often allowed much control over their lives, but they were still restricted legally. Women's work generally consisted of household or other domestically inclined tasks. Peasant women were usually responsible for caring for the household, child care, gardening, and animal husbandry near the house. They could supplement their household income by spinning or brewing at home. At harvest time, they were also expected to help with fieldwork.[[180]](#cite_note-Daily14-200) Townswomen, like peasant women, were responsible for the household and could also engage in trade. The trades that were open to women varied by country and period.[[181]](#cite_note-Daily177-201) Noblewomen were responsible for running a household and could occasionally be expected to handle estates in the absence of male relatives, but they were usually restricted from participation in military or government affairs. The only role open to women in the Church was that of [nuns](/wiki/Nun "Nun"), as they could not become priests.[[180]](#cite_note-Daily14-200)

In [central](/wiki/Central_Italy "Central Italy") and [northern Italy](/wiki/Northern_Italy "Northern Italy") and in [Flanders](/wiki/Flanders "Flanders"), the rise of towns that were, to a degree, self-governing stimulated economic growth and created an environment for new types of trade associations. Commercial cities on the shores of the Baltic entered into agreements known as the [Hanseatic League](/wiki/Hanseatic_League "Hanseatic League"). The Italian [Maritime republics](/wiki/Maritime_republics "Maritime republics") such as [Venice](/wiki/Venice "Venice"), [Genoa](/wiki/Genoa "Genoa"), and [Pisa](/wiki/Pisa "Pisa") expanded their trade throughout the Mediterranean.[[U]](#cite_note-203) Great [trading fairs](/wiki/Fair "Fair") were established and flourished in [northern France](/wiki/Champagne_fairs "Champagne fairs") during the period, allowing Italian and German merchants to trade with each other as well as local merchants.[[183]](#cite_note-Epstein82-204) In the late 13th century new land and sea routes to the Far East were pioneered, famously described in *[The Travels of Marco Polo](/wiki/The_Travels_of_Marco_Polo "The Travels of Marco Polo")* written by one of the traders, [Marco Polo](/wiki/Marco_Polo "Marco Polo") (d. 1324).[[184]](#cite_note-Barber60-205) Besides new trading opportunities, agricultural and technological improvements increased crop yields, which allowed the trade networks to expand.[[185]](#cite_note-Backman160-206) Rising trade brought new methods of dealing with money, and gold coinage was again minted in Europe, first in Italy and later in France and other countries. New forms of commercial contracts emerged, sharing risk among merchants. Accounting methods improved, partly through the use of [double-entry bookkeeping](/wiki/Double-entry_bookkeeping "Double-entry bookkeeping"); [letters of credit](/wiki/Letters_of_credit "Letters of credit") also appeared, allowing easy transmission of money.[[186]](#cite_note-Barber74-207)

### Rise of state power

Main articles: [England in the Middle Ages](/wiki/England_in_the_Middle_Ages "England in the Middle Ages"), [France in the Middle Ages](/wiki/France_in_the_Middle_Ages "France in the Middle Ages"), [Germany in the Middle Ages](/wiki/Germany_in_the_Middle_Ages "Germany in the Middle Ages"), [Italy in the Middle Ages](/wiki/Italy_in_the_Middle_Ages "Italy in the Middle Ages"), [Scotland in the Middle Ages](/wiki/Scotland_in_the_Middle_Ages "Scotland in the Middle Ages"), [Spain in the Middle Ages](/wiki/Spain_in_the_Middle_Ages "Spain in the Middle Ages"), and [Poland in the Middle Ages](/wiki/Poland_in_the_Middle_Ages "Poland in the Middle Ages")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/75/Europe_mediterranean_1190.jpg/330px-Europe_mediterranean_1190.jpg)](/wiki/File:Europe_mediterranean_1190.jpg)

Europe and the Mediterranean Sea in 1190

The High Middle Ages was the formative period in the history of the modern Western state. Kings in France, England, and Spain consolidated their power and established lasting governing institutions.[[187]](#cite_note-Backman283-208) New kingdoms such as [Hungary](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Hungary "Kingdom of Hungary") and [Poland](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Poland_(1025%E2%80%931385) "Kingdom of Poland (1025–1385)"), after their conversion to Christianity, became Central European powers.[[188]](#cite_note-Barber365-209) The Magyars settled Hungary around 900 under King [Árpád](/wiki/%C3%81rp%C3%A1d "Árpád") (d. c. 907) after a series of invasions in the 9th century.[[189]](#cite_note-Davies296-210) The papacy, long attached to an ideology of independence from secular kings, first asserted its claim to temporal authority over the entire Christian world; the [Papal Monarchy](/wiki/Temporal_power_(papal) "Temporal power (papal)") reached its apogee in the early 13th century under the pontificate of [Innocent III](/wiki/Innocent_III "Innocent III") (pope 1198–1216).[[190]](#cite_note-Backman262-211) [Northern Crusades](/wiki/Northern_Crusades "Northern Crusades") and the advance of Christian kingdoms and military orders into previously [pagan](/wiki/Pagan "Pagan") regions in the Baltic and [Finnic](/wiki/Finland "Finland") north-east brought the forced assimilation of numerous native peoples into European culture.[[191]](#cite_note-Barber371-212)

During the early High Middle Ages, Germany was ruled by the [Ottonian dynasty](/wiki/Ottonian_dynasty "Ottonian dynasty"), which struggled to control the powerful dukes ruling over [territorial duchies](/wiki/Stem_duchy "Stem duchy"), tracing back to the Migration period. In 1024, they were replaced by the [Salian dynasty](/wiki/Salian_dynasty "Salian dynasty"), who famously clashed with the papacy under Emperor [Henry IV](/wiki/Henry_IV,_Holy_Roman_Emperor "Henry IV, Holy Roman Emperor") (r. 1084–1105) over Church appointments as part of the [Investiture Controversy](/wiki/Investiture_Controversy "Investiture Controversy").[[192]](#cite_note-Backman181-213) His successors continued to struggle against the papacy as well as the German nobility. A period of instability followed the death of Emperor [Henry V](/wiki/Henry_V,_Holy_Roman_Emperor "Henry V, Holy Roman Emperor") (r. 1111–1125), who died without heirs, until [Frederick I Barbarossa](/wiki/Frederick_I_Barbarossa "Frederick I Barbarossa") (r. 1155–1190) took the imperial throne.[[193]](#cite_note-Jordan143-214) Although he ruled effectively, the basic problems remained, and his successors struggled into the 13th century.[[194]](#cite_note-Jordan250-215) Barbarossa's grandson [Frederick II](/wiki/Frederick_II,_Holy_Roman_Emperor "Frederick II, Holy Roman Emperor") (r. 1220–1250), who was also heir to the throne of Sicily through his mother, clashed repeatedly with the papacy. His court was famous for its scholars, and he was often accused of [heresy](/wiki/Heresy_in_Christianity "Heresy in Christianity").[[195]](#cite_note-Denley235-216) He and his successors faced many difficulties, including the invasion of the [Mongols](/wiki/Mongols "Mongols") into Europe in the mid-13th century. Mongols first shattered the principalities of Kievan Rus' and then [invaded Eastern Europe](/wiki/Mongol_invasion_of_Europe "Mongol invasion of Europe") in 1241, 1259, and 1287.[[196]](#cite_note-Davies364-217)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/76/Bayeux_Tapestry_scene44_William_Odo_Robert.jpg/250px-Bayeux_Tapestry_scene44_William_Odo_Robert.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bayeux_Tapestry_scene44_William_Odo_Robert.jpg)

The [Bayeux Tapestry](/wiki/Bayeux_Tapestry "Bayeux Tapestry") (detail) showing [William the Conqueror](/wiki/William_the_Conqueror "William the Conqueror") (centre), his half-brothers [Robert, Count of Mortain](/wiki/Robert,_Count_of_Mortain "Robert, Count of Mortain") (right) and [Odo](/wiki/Odo,_Earl_of_Kent "Odo, Earl of Kent"), Bishop of [Bayeux](/wiki/Bayeux "Bayeux") in the [Duchy of Normandy](/wiki/Duchy_of_Normandy "Duchy of Normandy") (left)

Under the [Capetian dynasty](/wiki/Capetian_dynasty "Capetian dynasty") the French monarchy slowly began to expand its authority over the nobility, growing out of the [Île-de-France](/wiki/%C3%8Ele-de-France "Île-de-France") to exert control over more of the country in the 11th and 12th centuries.[[197]](#cite_note-Backman187-218) They faced a powerful rival in the [Dukes of Normandy](/wiki/Dukes_of_Normandy "Dukes of Normandy"), who in 1066 under [William the Conqueror](/wiki/William_the_Conqueror "William the Conqueror") (duke 1035–1087), conquered England (r. 1066–1087) and created a cross-channel empire that lasted, in various forms, throughout the rest of the Middle Ages.[[198]](#cite_note-Jordan59-219)[[199]](#cite_note-Backman189-220) Normans also settled in Sicily and southern Italy, when [Robert Guiscard](/wiki/Robert_Guiscard "Robert Guiscard") (d. 1085) landed there in 1059 and established a duchy that later became the [Kingdom of Sicily](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Sicily "Kingdom of Sicily").[[200]](#cite_note-Davies294-221) Under the [Angevin dynasty](/wiki/Angevin_kings_of_England "Angevin kings of England") of [Henry II](/wiki/Henry_II_of_England "Henry II of England") (r. 1154–1189) and his son [Richard I](/wiki/Richard_I "Richard I") (r. 1189–1199), the kings of England ruled over England and large areas of France,[[201]](#cite_note-Backman263-222)[[V]](#cite_note-224) brought to the family by Henry II's marriage to [Eleanor of Aquitaine](/wiki/Eleanor_of_Aquitaine "Eleanor of Aquitaine") (d. 1204), heiress to much of southern France.[[203]](#cite_note-Eleanor-225)[[W]](#cite_note-226) Richard's younger brother [John](/wiki/John,_King_of_England "John, King of England") (r. 1199–1216) lost Normandy and the rest of the northern French possessions in 1204 to the French King [Philip II Augustus](/wiki/Philip_II_Augustus "Philip II Augustus") (r. 1180–1223). This led to dissension among the English nobility. John's financial exactions to pay for his unsuccessful attempts to regain Normandy led in 1215 to *[Magna Carta](/wiki/Magna_Carta "Magna Carta")*, a charter that confirmed the rights and privileges of free men in England. Under [Henry III](/wiki/Henry_III_of_England "Henry III of England") (r. 1216–1272), John's son, further concessions were made to the nobility, and royal power was diminished.[[204]](#cite_note-Backman286-227) The French monarchy continued to make gains against the nobility during the late 12th and 13th centuries, bringing more territories within the kingdom under the king's personal rule and centralising the royal administration.[[205]](#cite_note-Backman289-228) Under [Louis IX](/wiki/Louis_IX "Louis IX") (r. 1226–1270), royal prestige rose to new heights as Louis served as a mediator for most of Europe.[[206]](#cite_note-Davies355-229)[[X]](#cite_note-231)

In Iberia, the Christian states, which had been confined to the north-western part of the peninsula, began to push back against the Islamic states in the south, a period known as the *[Reconquista](/wiki/Reconquista "Reconquista")*.[[208]](#cite_note-Davies345-232) By about 1150, the Christian north had coalesced into the five major kingdoms of [León](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Le%C3%B3n "Kingdom of León"), [Castile](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Castile "Kingdom of Castile"), [Aragon](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Aragon "Kingdom of Aragon"), [Navarre](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Navarre "Kingdom of Navarre"), and [Portugal](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Portugal "Kingdom of Portugal").[[209]](#cite_note-Barber341-233) Southern Iberia remained under control of Islamic states, initially under the [Caliphate of Córdoba](/wiki/Caliphate_of_C%C3%B3rdoba "Caliphate of Córdoba"), which broke up in 1031 into a shifting number of petty states known as *[taifas](/wiki/Taifa "Taifa")*,[[208]](#cite_note-Davies345-232) who fought with the Christians until the [Almohad Caliphate](/wiki/Almohad_Caliphate "Almohad Caliphate") re-established centralised rule over Southern Iberia in the 1170s.[[210]](#cite_note-Barber350-234) Christian forces advanced again in the early 13th century, culminating in the capture of [Seville](/wiki/Seville "Seville") in 1248.[[211]](#cite_note-Barber353-235)

### Crusades

Main articles: [Crusades](/wiki/Crusades "Crusades"), [Reconquista](/wiki/Reconquista "Reconquista"), and [Northern Crusades](/wiki/Northern_Crusades "Northern Crusades")

See also: [Byzantine Empire under the Macedonian dynasty](/wiki/Byzantine_Empire_under_the_Macedonian_dynasty "Byzantine Empire under the Macedonian dynasty") and [Byzantine Empire under the Komnenos dynasty](/wiki/Byzantine_Empire_under_the_Komnenos_dynasty "Byzantine Empire under the Komnenos dynasty")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5a/Crac_des_chevaliers_syria.jpeg/330px-Crac_des_chevaliers_syria.jpeg)](/wiki/File:Crac_des_chevaliers_syria.jpeg)

[Krak des Chevaliers](/wiki/Krak_des_Chevaliers "Krak des Chevaliers") was built during the Crusades for the [Knights Hospitallers](/wiki/Knights_Hospitaller "Knights Hospitaller").[[212]](#cite_note-Fortress268-236)

In the 11th century, the [Seljuk Turks](/wiki/Seljuk_Turks "Seljuk Turks") took over much of the Middle East, occupying Persia during the 1040s, Armenia in the 1060s, and Jerusalem in 1070. In 1071, the Turkish army defeated the Byzantine army at the [Battle of Manzikert](/wiki/Battle_of_Manzikert "Battle of Manzikert") and captured the Byzantine Emperor [Romanus IV](/wiki/Romanus_IV "Romanus IV") (r. 1068–1071). The Turks were then free to invade Asia Minor, which dealt a dangerous blow to the Byzantine Empire by seizing a large part of its population and its economic heartland. Although the Byzantines regrouped and recovered somewhat, they never fully regained Asia Minor and were often on the defensive. The Turks also had difficulties, losing control of Jerusalem to the [Fatimids](/wiki/Fatimids "Fatimids") of Egypt and suffering from a series of internal civil wars.[[213]](#cite_note-Davies332-237) The Byzantines also faced a revived [Bulgaria](/wiki/Second_Bulgarian_Empire "Second Bulgarian Empire"), which in the late 12th and 13th centuries spread throughout the Balkans.[[214]](#cite_note-Davies386-238)

The Crusades were intended to seize [Jerusalem](/wiki/Jerusalem "Jerusalem") from Muslim control. The [First Crusade](/wiki/First_Crusade "First Crusade") was proclaimed by Pope [Urban II](/wiki/Urban_II "Urban II") (pope 1088–1099) at the [Council of Clermont](/wiki/Council_of_Clermont "Council of Clermont") in 1095 in response to a request from the Byzantine Emperor [Alexios I Komnenos](/wiki/Alexios_I_Komnenos "Alexios I Komnenos") (r. 1081–1118) for aid against further Muslim advances. Urban promised [indulgence](/wiki/Indulgence "Indulgence") to anyone who took part. Tens of thousands of people from all levels of society mobilised across Europe and captured Jerusalem in 1099.[[215]](#cite_note-MACrusades-239) One feature of the crusades was the [pogroms](/wiki/Pogrom "Pogrom") against local Jews that often took place as the crusaders left their countries for the East. These were especially brutal during the First Crusade,[[80]](#cite_note-Jews191-87) when the Jewish communities in [Cologne](/wiki/Cologne "Cologne"), [Mainz](/wiki/Mainz "Mainz"), and [Worms](/wiki/Worms,_Germany "Worms, Germany") were destroyed, as well as other communities in cities between the rivers [Seine](/wiki/Seine "Seine") and the Rhine.[[216]](#cite_note-Comp397-240) Another outgrowth of the crusades was the foundation of a new type of monastic order, the [military orders](/wiki/Military_order_(society) "Military order (society)") of the [Templars](/wiki/Templars "Templars") and [Hospitallers](/wiki/Hospitallers "Hospitallers"), which fused monastic life with military service.[[217]](#cite_note-Barber145-241)

The Crusaders consolidated their conquests into [Crusader states](/wiki/Crusader_states "Crusader states"). During the 12th and 13th centuries, there were a series of conflicts between them and the surrounding Islamic states. Appeals from the crusader states to the papacy led to further crusades,[[215]](#cite_note-MACrusades-239) such as the [Third Crusade](/wiki/Third_Crusade "Third Crusade"), called to try to regain Jerusalem, which had been captured by [Saladin](/wiki/Saladin "Saladin") (d. 1193) in 1187.[[218]](#cite_note-Payne204-242)[[Y]](#cite_note-244) In 1203, the [Fourth Crusade](/wiki/Fourth_Crusade "Fourth Crusade") was diverted from the Holy Land to Constantinople, and captured the city in 1204, setting up a [Latin Empire of Constantinople](/wiki/Latin_Empire_of_Constantinople "Latin Empire of Constantinople")[[220]](#cite_note-Lock156-245) and greatly weakening the Byzantine Empire. The Byzantines recaptured the city in 1261 but never regained their former strength.[[221]](#cite_note-Backman299-246) By 1291, all the crusader states had been captured or forced from the mainland. However, a titular [Kingdom of Jerusalem](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Jerusalem "Kingdom of Jerusalem") survived on the island of [Cyprus](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Cyprus "Kingdom of Cyprus") for several years afterwards.[[222]](#cite_note-Lock122-247)

Popes called for crusades elsewhere besides the Holy Land: in Spain, southern France, and along the Baltic.[[215]](#cite_note-MACrusades-239) The Spanish crusades became fused with the *Reconquista* of Spain from the Muslims. Although the Templars and Hospitallers took part in the Spanish crusades, similar Spanish military religious orders were founded, most of which had become part of the two main orders of [Calatrava](/wiki/Order_of_Calatrava "Order of Calatrava") and [Santiago](/wiki/Order_of_Santiago "Order of Santiago") by the beginning of the 12th century.[[223]](#cite_note-Lock205-248) Northern Europe also remained outside Christian influence until the 11th century or later and became a crusading venue as part of the Northern Crusades of the 12th to 14th centuries. These crusades also spawned a military order, the [Order of the Sword Brothers](/wiki/Order_of_the_Sword_Brothers "Order of the Sword Brothers"). Another order, the [Teutonic Knights](/wiki/Teutonic_Knights "Teutonic Knights"), although founded in the crusader states, focused much of its activity in the Baltic after 1225 and, in 1309, moved its headquarters to [Marienburg](/wiki/Malbork_Castle "Malbork Castle") in [Prussia](/wiki/Prussia "Prussia").[[224]](#cite_note-Lock213-249)

### Intellectual life

Main articles: [Renaissance of the 12th century](/wiki/Renaissance_of_the_12th_century "Renaissance of the 12th century"), [Medieval philosophy](/wiki/Medieval_philosophy "Medieval philosophy"), [Medieval literature](/wiki/Medieval_literature "Medieval literature"), [Medieval poetry](/wiki/Medieval_poetry "Medieval poetry"), and [Medieval medicine of Western Europe](/wiki/Medieval_medicine_of_Western_Europe "Medieval medicine of Western Europe")

During the 11th century, developments in philosophy and theology led to increased intellectual activity. There was a debate between the [realists](/wiki/Philosophical_realism "Philosophical realism") and the [nominalists](/wiki/Nominalists "Nominalists") over the concept of "[universals](/wiki/Universals "Universals")". Philosophical discourse was stimulated by the rediscovery of [Aristotle](/wiki/Aristotle "Aristotle") and his emphasis on [empiricism](/wiki/Empiricism "Empiricism") and [rationalism](/wiki/Rationalism "Rationalism"). Scholars such as [Peter Abelard](/wiki/Peter_Abelard "Peter Abelard") (d. 1142) and [Peter Lombard](/wiki/Peter_Lombard "Peter Lombard") (d. 1164) introduced [Aristotelian logic](/wiki/Aristotelian_logic "Aristotelian logic") into theology. In the late 11th and early 12th centuries [cathedral schools](/wiki/Cathedral_school "Cathedral school") spread throughout Western Europe, signalling the shift of learning from monasteries to cathedrals and towns.[[225]](#cite_note-Backman232-250) Cathedral schools were in turn replaced by the [universities](/wiki/Medieval_university "Medieval university") established in major European cities.[[226]](#cite_note-Backman247-251) Philosophy and theology fused in [scholasticism](/wiki/Scholasticism "Scholasticism"), an attempt by 12th- and 13th-century scholars to reconcile authoritative texts, most notably Aristotle and the Bible. This movement tried to employ a systemic approach to truth and reason[[227]](#cite_note-MASchol-252) and culminated in the thought of [Thomas Aquinas](/wiki/Thomas_Aquinas "Thomas Aquinas") (d. 1274), who wrote the *[Summa Theologica](/wiki/Summa_Theologica "Summa Theologica")*, or *Summary of Theology*.[[228]](#cite_note-Colish295-253)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bd/Richard_of_Wallingford.jpg/250px-Richard_of_Wallingford.jpg)](/wiki/File:Richard_of_Wallingford.jpg)

A medieval scholar making precise measurements in a 14th-century manuscript illustration

[Chivalry](/wiki/Chivalry "Chivalry") and the ethos of [courtly love](/wiki/Courtly_love "Courtly love") developed in royal and noble courts. This culture was expressed in the [vernacular languages](/wiki/Vernacular_languages "Vernacular languages") rather than Latin and comprised poems, stories, legends, and popular songs spread by [troubadours](/wiki/Troubadour "Troubadour") or [Minnesängers](/wiki/Minnes%C3%A4nger "Minnesänger"), or wandering minstrels. Often the stories were written down in the *[chansons de geste](/wiki/Chansons_de_geste "Chansons de geste")*, or "songs of great deeds", such as *[The Song of Roland](/wiki/The_Song_of_Roland "The Song of Roland")* or *[The Song of Hildebrand](/wiki/Hildebrand "Hildebrand")*.[[229]](#cite_note-Backman252-254) Secular and religious histories were also produced.[[230]](#cite_note-Davies349-255) [Geoffrey of Monmouth](/wiki/Geoffrey_of_Monmouth "Geoffrey of Monmouth") (d. c. 1155) composed his *[Historia Regum Britanniae](/wiki/Historia_Regum_Britanniae "Historia Regum Britanniae")*, a collection of stories and legends about [Arthur](/wiki/King_Arthur "King Arthur").[[231]](#cite_note-SaulGeoffrey-256) Other works were more clearly history, such as [Otto von Freising](/wiki/Otto_von_Freising "Otto von Freising")'s (d. 1158) *Gesta Friderici Imperatoris* detailing the deeds of Emperor Frederick Barbarossa, or [William of Malmesbury](/wiki/William_of_Malmesbury "William of Malmesbury")'s (d. c. 1143) *Gesta Regum* on the kings of England.[[230]](#cite_note-Davies349-255)

Legal studies advanced during the 12th century. Both secular law and [canon law](/wiki/Canon_law "Canon law"), or ecclesiastical law, were studied in the High Middle Ages. Secular law, or Roman law, was significantly advanced by the discovery of the *[Corpus Juris Civilis](/wiki/Corpus_Juris_Civilis "Corpus Juris Civilis")* in the 11th century, and by 1100, Roman law was being taught at [Bologna](/wiki/University_of_Bologna "University of Bologna"). This led to the recording and standardisation of legal codes throughout Western Europe. Canon law was also studied, and around 1140, a monk named [Gratian](/wiki/Decretum_Gratiani "Decretum Gratiani") (fl. 12th century), a teacher at Bologna, wrote what became the standard text of canon law—the *[Decretum](/wiki/Decretum_Gratiani "Decretum Gratiani")*.[[232]](#cite_note-Backman237-257)

Among the results of the Greek and Islamic influence on this period in European history was the replacement of [Roman numerals](/wiki/Roman_numerals "Roman numerals") with the [decimal](/wiki/Decimal "Decimal") [positional number system](/wiki/Positional_number_system "Positional number system") and the invention of [algebra](/wiki/Algebra "Algebra"), which allowed more advanced mathematics. Astronomy advanced following the translation of [Ptolemy](/wiki/Ptolemy "Ptolemy")'s *[Almagest](/wiki/Almagest "Almagest")* from Greek into Latin in the late 12th century. Medicine was also studied, especially in southern Italy, where Islamic medicine influenced the [school at Salerno](/wiki/Schola_Medica_Salernitana "Schola Medica Salernitana").[[233]](#cite_note-Backman241-258)

### Technology and military

Main articles: [Medieval technology](/wiki/Medieval_technology "Medieval technology"), [Medieval warfare](/wiki/Medieval_warfare "Medieval warfare"), and [History of science § Science in the Middle Ages](/wiki/History_of_science#Science_in_the_Middle_Ages "History of science")

Further information: [List of medieval European scientists](/wiki/List_of_medieval_European_scientists "List of medieval European scientists")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/84/Tommaso_da_modena%2C_ritratti_di_domenicani_%28Ugo_di_Provenza%29_1352_150cm%2C_treviso%2C_ex_convento_di_san_niccol%C3%B2%2C_sala_del_capitolo.jpg/250px-Tommaso_da_modena%2C_ritratti_di_domenicani_%28Ugo_di_Provenza%29_1352_150cm%2C_treviso%2C_ex_convento_di_san_niccol%C3%B2%2C_sala_del_capitolo.jpg)](/wiki/File:Tommaso_da_modena,_ritratti_di_domenicani_(Ugo_di_Provenza)_1352_150cm,_treviso,_ex_convento_di_san_niccol%C3%B2,_sala_del_capitolo.jpg)

Portrait of Cardinal [Hugh of Saint-Cher](/wiki/Hugh_of_Saint-Cher "Hugh of Saint-Cher") by [Tommaso da Modena](/wiki/Tommaso_da_Modena "Tommaso da Modena"), 1352, the first known depiction of [spectacles](/wiki/Spectacles "Spectacles")[[234]](#cite_note-259)

In the 12th and 13th centuries, Europe experienced economic growth and innovations in methods of production. Significant technological advances included the invention of the [windmill](/wiki/Windmill "Windmill"), the first mechanical clocks, the manufacture of [distilled spirits](/wiki/Distilled_spirits "Distilled spirits"), and the use of the [astrolabe](/wiki/Astrolabe "Astrolabe").[[235]](#cite_note-Backman246-260) Concave spectacles were invented around 1286 by an unknown Italian artisan, probably working in or near Pisa.[[236]](#cite_note-261)

The development of a three-field [rotation system](/wiki/Crop_rotation "Crop rotation") for planting crops[[163]](#cite_note-Backman156-179)[[Z]](#cite_note-263) increased the usage of land from one-half in use each year under the old two-field system to two-thirds under the new system, with a consequent increase in production.[[237]](#cite_note-Epstein45-262) The development of the [heavy plough](/wiki/Plough "Plough") allowed heavier soils to be farmed more efficiently, aided by the spread of the [horse collar](/wiki/Horse_collar "Horse collar"), which led to the use of [draught horses](/wiki/Working_animal "Working animal") in place of oxen. Horses are faster than oxen and require less pasture, factors that aided the implementation of the three-field system.[[238]](#cite_note-Backman156-59-264) Legumes – such as peas, beans, or lentils – were grown more widely as crops, in addition to the usual cereal crops of wheat, oats, barley, and rye.[[239]](#cite_note-Barber80-265)

The construction of [cathedrals](/wiki/Cathedral "Cathedral") and castles advanced building technology, developing large stone buildings. Ancillary structures included new town halls, houses, bridges, and [tithe barns](/wiki/Tithe_barn "Tithe barn").[[240]](#cite_note-Barber68-266) Shipbuilding improved with the use of the [rib and plank](/wiki/Boat_building "Boat building") method rather than the old Roman system of [mortise and tenon](/wiki/Mortise_and_tenon "Mortise and tenon"). Other improvements to ships included the use of [lateen](/wiki/Lateen "Lateen") sails and the [stern-post rudder](/wiki/Rudder#Medieval_Europe "Rudder"), both of which increased the speed at which ships could be sailed.[[241]](#cite_note-Barber73-267)

In military affairs, the use of infantry with specialised roles increased. Along with the still-dominant heavy cavalry, armies often included mounted and infantry [crossbowmen](/wiki/Crossbowmen "Crossbowmen"), as well as [sappers](/wiki/Sapper "Sapper") and engineers.[[242]](#cite_note-NicolleWestern125-268) Crossbows, which had been known in Late Antiquity, increased in use partly because of the increase in [siege](/wiki/Siege "Siege") warfare in the 10th and 11th centuries.[[158]](#cite_note-NicolleWestern80-174)[[AA]](#cite_note-270) The increasing use of crossbows during the 12th and 13th centuries led to the use of closed-face [helmets](/wiki/Combat_helmet "Combat helmet"), heavy body armour, as well as [horse armour](/wiki/Horse_armour "Horse armour").[[244]](#cite_note-NicolleWestern130-271) [Gunpowder](/wiki/Gunpowder "Gunpowder") was known in Europe by the mid-13th century with a recorded use in European warfare by the English against the Scots in 1304. However, it was merely used as an explosive and not as a weapon. [Cannon](/wiki/Cannon "Cannon") were being used for sieges in the 1320s, and hand-held guns were in use by the 1360s.[[245]](#cite_note-Nicolle296-272)

### Architecture, art, and music

Further information: [Medieval architecture](/wiki/Medieval_architecture "Medieval architecture"), [Medieval art](/wiki/Medieval_art "Medieval art"), and [Medieval music](/wiki/Medieval_music "Medieval music")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/94/Maria_Lach_02.jpg/250px-Maria_Lach_02.jpg)](/wiki/File:Maria_Lach_02.jpg)

The [Romanesque](/wiki/Romanesque_architecture "Romanesque architecture") Church of [Maria Laach](/wiki/Maria_Laach_Abbey "Maria Laach Abbey"), Germany

In the 10th century, the establishment of churches and monasteries led to the development of stone architecture that elaborated vernacular Roman forms, from which the term "Romanesque" was derived. Where available, Roman [brick](/wiki/Brick "Brick") and stone buildings were recycled for their materials. From the tentative beginnings known as the [First Romanesque](/wiki/First_Romanesque "First Romanesque"), the style flourished and spread across Europe in a remarkably homogeneous form. Just before 1000, a great wave of stone churches were being built all over Europe.[[246]](#cite_note-Benton55-273) [Romanesque](/wiki/Romanesque_architecture "Romanesque architecture") buildings have massive stone walls, openings topped by semi-circular arches, small windows, and, particularly in France, arched stone vaults.[[247]](#cite_note-Adams181-274) The large [portal](/wiki/Portal_(architecture) "Portal (architecture)") with coloured sculpture in [high relief](/wiki/Relief "Relief") became a central feature of façades, especially in France, and the [capitals](/wiki/Capital_(architecture) "Capital (architecture)") of columns were often carved with narrative scenes of imaginative monsters and animals.[[248]](#cite_note-Benton58to75-275) According to art historian [C. R. Dodwell](/wiki/C._R._Dodwell "C. R. Dodwell"), "virtually all the churches in the West were decorated with wall-paintings", of which few survive.[[249]](#cite_note-Dodwell37-276) Simultaneous with the development in church architecture, the distinctive European form of the castle was developed and became crucial to politics and warfare.[[250]](#cite_note-Benton295-277)

Romanesque art, especially metalwork, was at its most sophisticated in [Mosan art](/wiki/Mosan_art "Mosan art"), in which distinct artistic personalities, including [Nicholas of Verdun](/wiki/Nicholas_of_Verdun "Nicholas of Verdun") (d. 1205), become apparent. An almost [classical style](/wiki/Ancient_Greek_art "Ancient Greek art") is seen in works such as a [font at Liège](/wiki/Baptismal_font_at_St_Bartholomew%27s_Church,_Li%C3%A8ge "Baptismal font at St Bartholomew's Church, Liège"),[[251]](#cite_note-Lasko240-278) contrasting with the writhing animals of the exactly contemporary [Gloucester Candlestick](/wiki/Gloucester_Candlestick "Gloucester Candlestick"). Large illuminated bibles and [psalters](/wiki/Psalter "Psalter") were the typical forms of luxury manuscripts, and wall-painting flourished in churches, often following a scheme with a *[Last Judgement](/wiki/Last_Judgement "Last Judgement")* on the west wall, a [Christ in Majesty](/wiki/Christ_in_Majesty "Christ in Majesty") at the east end, and narrative biblical scenes down the nave, or in the best surviving example, at [Saint-Savin-sur-Gartempe](/wiki/Abbey_Church_of_Saint-Savin-sur-Gartempe "Abbey Church of Saint-Savin-sur-Gartempe"), on the [barrel-vaulted](/wiki/Barrel-vault "Barrel-vault") roof.[[252]](#cite_note-Benton91-279)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/05/Nef_cath%C3%A9drale_Laon.jpg/250px-Nef_cath%C3%A9drale_Laon.jpg)](/wiki/File:Nef_cath%C3%A9drale_Laon.jpg)

The [Gothic](/wiki/Gothic_architecture "Gothic architecture") interior of [Laon Cathedral](/wiki/Laon_Cathedral "Laon Cathedral"), France

From the early 12th century, French builders developed the [Gothic](/wiki/Gothic_architecture "Gothic architecture") style, marked by the use of [rib vaults](/wiki/Rib_vault "Rib vault"), [pointed arches](/wiki/Ogive "Ogive"), [flying buttresses](/wiki/Flying_buttress "Flying buttress"), and large [stained glass](/wiki/Stained_glass "Stained glass") windows. It was used mainly in churches and cathedrals and continued until the 16th century in much of Europe. Classic examples of Gothic architecture include [Chartres Cathedral](/wiki/Chartres_Cathedral "Chartres Cathedral") and [Reims Cathedral](/wiki/Reims_Cathedral "Reims Cathedral") in France, as well as [Salisbury Cathedral](/wiki/Salisbury_Cathedral "Salisbury Cathedral") in England.[[253]](#cite_note-Adams195-280) Stained glass became a crucial element in the design of churches, which continued to use extensive wall-paintings, now almost all lost.[[254]](#cite_note-Benton185to271-281)

During this period, the practice of manuscript illumination gradually passed from monasteries to lay workshops, so that according to [Janetta Benton](/wiki/Janetta_Benton "Janetta Benton") "by 1300 most monks bought their books in shops",[[255]](#cite_note-Benton250-282) and the [book of hours](/wiki/Book_of_hours "Book of hours") developed as a form of devotional book for lay-people. Metalwork remained the most prestigious art form, with [Limoges enamel](/wiki/Limoges_enamel "Limoges enamel") a popular and relatively affordable option for objects such as reliquaries and crosses.[[256]](#cite_note-Benton245-283) In Italy the innovations of [Cimabue](/wiki/Cimabue "Cimabue") and [Duccio](/wiki/Duccio "Duccio"), followed by the [Trecento](/wiki/Trecento "Trecento") master [Giotto](/wiki/Giotto "Giotto") (d. 1337), greatly increased the sophistication and status of [panel painting](/wiki/Panel_painting "Panel painting") and [fresco](/wiki/Fresco "Fresco").[[257]](#cite_note-Benton264-284) Increasing prosperity during the 12th century resulted in greater production of secular art; many [carved ivory](/wiki/Carved_ivory "Carved ivory") objects such as gaming-pieces, combs, and small religious figures have survived.[[258]](#cite_note-Benton248-285)

### Church life

Main articles: [Gregorian Reform](/wiki/Gregorian_Reform "Gregorian Reform") and [Church and state in medieval Europe](/wiki/Church_and_state_in_medieval_Europe "Church and state in medieval Europe")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a3/Bonaventura_Berlinghieri_Francesco.jpg/250px-Bonaventura_Berlinghieri_Francesco.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bonaventura_Berlinghieri_Francesco.jpg)

[Francis of Assisi](/wiki/Francis_of_Assisi "Francis of Assisi"), depicted by [Bonaventura Berlinghieri](/wiki/Bonaventura_Berlinghieri "Bonaventura Berlinghieri") in 1235, founded the [Franciscan](/wiki/Franciscan "Franciscan") Order.[[259]](#cite_note-Hamilton47-286)

Monastic reform became an important issue during the 11th century, as elites began to worry that monks were not adhering to the rules binding them to a strictly religious life. [Cluny Abbey](/wiki/Cluny_Abbey "Cluny Abbey"), founded in the [Mâcon](/wiki/M%C3%A2con "Mâcon") region of France in 909, was established as part of the [Cluniac Reforms](/wiki/Cluniac_Reforms "Cluniac Reforms"), a larger movement of monastic reform in response to this fear.[[260]](#cite_note-Rhino-287) Cluny quickly established a reputation for austerity and rigour. It sought to maintain a high quality of spiritual life by placing itself under the protection of the papacy and by electing its own abbot without interference from laymen, thus maintaining economic and political independence from local lords.[[261]](#cite_note-Barber143-288)

Monastic reform inspired change in the secular Church. The ideals upon which it was based were brought to the papacy by Pope [Leo IX](/wiki/Leo_IX "Leo IX") (pope 1049–1054) and provided the ideology of clerical independence that led to the Investiture Controversy in the late 11th century. This involved Pope [Gregory VII](/wiki/Pope_Gregory_VII "Pope Gregory VII") (pope 1073–1085) and Emperor Henry IV, who initially clashed over episcopal appointments, a dispute that turned into a battle over the ideas of [investiture](/wiki/Investiture "Investiture"), clerical marriage, and [simony](/wiki/Simony "Simony"). The emperor saw the protection of the Church as one of his responsibilities and wanted to preserve the right to appoint his own choices as bishops within his lands. Still, the papacy insisted on the Church's independence from secular lords. These issues remained unresolved after the compromise of 1122, known as the [Concordat of Worms](/wiki/Concordat_of_Worms "Concordat of Worms"). The dispute represents a significant stage in creating a papal monarchy separate from and equal to [lay](/wiki/Laity "Laity") authorities. It also had the permanent consequence of empowering German princes at the expense of the German emperors.[[260]](#cite_note-Rhino-287)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e9/Abbey-of-senanque-provence-gordes.jpg/250px-Abbey-of-senanque-provence-gordes.jpg)](/wiki/File:Abbey-of-senanque-provence-gordes.jpg)

[Sénanque Abbey](/wiki/S%C3%A9nanque_Abbey "Sénanque Abbey"), [Gordes](/wiki/Gordes "Gordes"), France

The High Middle Ages was a period of great religious movements. Besides the Crusades and monastic reforms, people sought to participate in new forms of religious life. New monastic orders were founded, including the [Carthusians](/wiki/Carthusian "Carthusian") and the [Cistercians](/wiki/Cistercians "Cistercians"). The latter, in particular, expanded rapidly in their early years under the guidance of [Bernard of Clairvaux](/wiki/Bernard_of_Clairvaux "Bernard of Clairvaux") (d. 1153). These new orders were formed in response to the feeling of the laity that Benedictine monasticism no longer met the needs of the laymen, who, along with those wishing to enter the religious life, wanted a return to the simpler [hermetical](/wiki/Hermit "Hermit") monasticism of early Christianity, or to live an [Apostolic life](/wiki/Apostles_in_the_New_Testament "Apostles in the New Testament").[[217]](#cite_note-Barber145-241) [Religious pilgrimages](/wiki/Christian_pilgrimage "Christian pilgrimage") were also encouraged. Old pilgrimage sites such as Rome, Jerusalem, and [Compostela](/wiki/Cathedral_of_Santiago_de_Compostela "Cathedral of Santiago de Compostela") received increasing numbers of visitors, and new sites such as [Monte Gargano](/wiki/Sanctuary_of_Monte_Sant%27Angelo "Sanctuary of Monte Sant'Angelo") and [Bari](/wiki/Basilica_di_San_Nicola "Basilica di San Nicola") rose to prominence.[[262]](#cite_note-Morris199-289)

In the 13th century [mendicant orders](/wiki/Mendicant_orders "Mendicant orders")—the [Franciscans](/wiki/Franciscan "Franciscan") and the [Dominicans](/wiki/Dominican_Order "Dominican Order")—who swore vows of poverty and earned their living by begging, were approved by the papacy.[[263]](#cite_note-Barber155-290) Religious groups such as the [Waldensians](/wiki/Waldensians "Waldensians") and the [Humiliati](/wiki/Humiliati "Humiliati") also attempted to return to the life of early Christianity in the middle 12th and early 13th centuries, another heretical movement condemned by the papacy. Others joined the [Cathars](/wiki/Cathars "Cathars"), another movement condemned as heretical by the papacy. In 1209, a crusade was preached against the Cathars, the [Albigensian Crusade](/wiki/Albigensian_Crusade "Albigensian Crusade"), which, in combination with the [medieval Inquisition](/wiki/Medieval_Inquisition "Medieval Inquisition"), eliminated them.[[264]](#cite_note-Barber185-291)

## Late Middle Ages

Main article: [Late Middle Ages](/wiki/Late_Middle_Ages "Late Middle Ages")

### War, famine, and plague

Main article: [Crisis of the Late Middle Ages](/wiki/Crisis_of_the_Late_Middle_Ages "Crisis of the Late Middle Ages")

The first years of the 14th century were marked by famines, culminating in the [Great Famine of 1315–1317](/wiki/Great_Famine_of_1315%E2%80%931317 "Great Famine of 1315–1317").[[265]](#cite_note-MAFamine-292) The causes of the Great Famine included the slow transition from the [Medieval Warm Period](/wiki/Medieval_Warm_Period "Medieval Warm Period") to the [Little Ice Age](/wiki/Little_Ice_Age "Little Ice Age"), which left the population vulnerable when bad weather caused crop failures.[[266]](#cite_note-Backman373-293) The years 1313–1314 and 1317–1321 were excessively rainy throughout Europe, resulting in widespread crop failures.[[267]](#cite_note-Epstein41-294) The climate change—which resulted in a declining average annual temperature for Europe during the 14th century—was accompanied by an economic downturn.[[268]](#cite_note-Backman370-295)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/47/Jacquerie_Navarre.jpg/250px-Jacquerie_Navarre.jpg)](/wiki/File:Jacquerie_Navarre.jpg)

Execution of some of the ringleaders of the *[jacquerie](/wiki/Jacquerie "Jacquerie")*, from a 14th-century manuscript of the *Chroniques de France ou de St Denis*

These troubles were followed in 1347 by the [Black Death](/wiki/Black_Death "Black Death"), a [pandemic](/wiki/Pandemic "Pandemic") that spread throughout Europe during the following three years.[[269]](#cite_note-MAPlague-296)[[AB]](#cite_note-298) The death toll was probably about 35 million people in Europe, about one-third of the population. Towns were especially hard-hit because of their crowded conditions.[[AC]](#cite_note-300) Large areas of land were left sparsely inhabited, and in some places fields were left unworked. Wages rose as landlords sought to entice fewer available workers to their fields. Further problems were lower rents and lower demand for food, which cut into agricultural income. Urban workers also felt they had a right to greater earnings, and [popular uprisings](/wiki/Popular_revolt_in_late-medieval_Europe "Popular revolt in late-medieval Europe") broke out across Europe.[[272]](#cite_note-Backman374-301) Among the uprisings were the *[jacquerie](/wiki/Jacquerie "Jacquerie")* in France, the [Peasants' Revolt](/wiki/Peasants%27_Revolt "Peasants' Revolt") in England, and revolts in the cities of [Florence](/wiki/Florence "Florence") in Italy and [Ghent](/wiki/Ghent "Ghent") and [Bruges](/wiki/Bruges "Bruges") in Flanders. The trauma of the plague led to an increased piety throughout Europe, manifested by the foundation of new charities, the self-mortification of the [flagellants](/wiki/Flagellant "Flagellant"), and the [scapegoating of Jews](/wiki/Black_Death_Jewish_persecutions "Black Death Jewish persecutions").[[273]](#cite_note-Davies412-302) Conditions were further unsettled by the return of the plague throughout the rest of the 14th century; it continued to strike Europe periodically during the rest of the Middle Ages.[[269]](#cite_note-MAPlague-296)

### Society and economy

Society throughout Europe was disturbed by the dislocations caused by the Black Death. Lands that had been marginally productive were abandoned as the survivors could acquire more fertile areas.[[274]](#cite_note-Epstein184-303) Although [serfdom](/wiki/Serfdom "Serfdom") declined in Western Europe, it became more common in Eastern Europe, as landlords imposed it on those of their tenants who had previously been free.[[275]](#cite_note-Epstein246-304) Most peasants in Western Europe changed the work they had previously owed to their landlords into cash rents.[[276]](#cite_note-Keen234-305) The percentage of serfs among the peasantry declined from a high of 90 to closer to 50 percent by the end of the period.[[172]](#cite_note-Singman8-190) Landlords also became more conscious of common interests with other landholders and joined to extort their governments' privileges. Partly at the urging of landlords, governments attempted to legislate a return to the economic conditions that existed before the Black Death.[[276]](#cite_note-Keen234-305) Non-clergy became increasingly literate, and urban populations began to imitate the nobility's interest in chivalry.[[277]](#cite_note-Vale346-306)

Jewish communities were [expelled from England](/wiki/Edict_of_Expulsion "Edict of Expulsion") in 1290 and from [France in 1306](/wiki/History_of_the_Jews_in_France#The_Great_Exile_of_1306 "History of the Jews in France"). Although some were allowed back into France, most were not. Many Jews emigrated eastwards, [settling in Poland](/wiki/History_of_the_Jews_in_Poland#Early_history:_966–1385 "History of the Jews in Poland") and Hungary.[[278]](#cite_note-Jews192-307) The Jews were expelled from [Spain in 1492](/wiki/Alhambra_Decree "Alhambra Decree"), and dispersed to Turkey, France, Italy, and Holland.[[80]](#cite_note-Jews191-87) The [rise of banking](/wiki/History_of_banking#Medieval_Europe "History of banking") in Italy during the 13th century continued throughout the 14th century, fuelled partly by the increasing warfare of the period and the needs of the papacy to move money between kingdoms. Many banking firms loaned money to royalty at great risk, as some were bankrupted when kings defaulted on their loans.[[279]](#cite_note-Keen237-308)[[AD]](#cite_note-309)

### State resurgence

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ed/Europe_1360.jpg/500px-Europe_1360.jpg)](/wiki/File:Europe_1360.jpg)

Europe in 1360

Strong, royalty-based [nation states](/wiki/Nation_state "Nation state") rose throughout Europe in the Late Middle Ages, particularly in [England](/wiki/Kingdom_of_England "Kingdom of England"), [France](/wiki/Kingdom_of_France "Kingdom of France"), and the Christian kingdoms of the Iberian Peninsula: [Aragon](/wiki/Crown_of_Aragon "Crown of Aragon"), [Castile](/wiki/Crown_of_Castile "Crown of Castile"), and [Portugal](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Portugal "Kingdom of Portugal"). The long conflicts of the period strengthened royal control over their kingdoms and were extremely hard on the peasantry. Kings profited from warfare that extended royal legislation and increased the lands they directly controlled.[[280]](#cite_note-Watts201-310) Paying for the wars required that methods of taxation become more effective and efficient, and the rate of taxation often increased.[[281]](#cite_note-Watts224-311) The requirement to obtain the consent of taxpayers allowed representative bodies such as the [English Parliament](/wiki/English_Parliament "English Parliament") and the [French Estates General](/wiki/French_Estates_General "French Estates General") to gain power and authority.[[282]](#cite_note-Watts233-312)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6e/Contemporaine_afb_jeanne_d_arc.png/250px-Contemporaine_afb_jeanne_d_arc.png)](/wiki/File:Contemporaine_afb_jeanne_d_arc.png)

[Joan of Arc](/wiki/Joan_of_Arc "Joan of Arc") in a 15th-century depiction

Throughout the 14th century, French kings sought to expand their influence at the expense of the territorial holdings of the nobility.[[283]](#cite_note-Watts166-313) They ran into difficulties when attempting to confiscate the holdings of the English kings in southern France, leading to the [Hundred Years' War](/wiki/Hundred_Years%27_War "Hundred Years' War"),[[284]](#cite_note-Watts169-314) waged from 1337 to 1453.[[285]](#cite_note-MA100-315) Early in the war the English under [Edward III](/wiki/Edward_III "Edward III") (r. 1327–1377) and his son [Edward, the Black Prince](/wiki/Edward,_the_Black_Prince "Edward, the Black Prince") (d. 1376),[[AE]](#cite_note-317) won the battles of [Crécy](/wiki/Cr%C3%A9cy "Crécy") and [Poitiers](/wiki/Battle_of_Poitiers "Battle of Poitiers"), captured the city of [Calais](/wiki/Calais "Calais"), and won control of much of France.[[AF]](#cite_note-319) The resulting stresses almost caused the disintegration of the French kingdom during the early years of the war.[[288]](#cite_note-Watts180-320) In the early 15th century, France again came close to dissolving, but in the late 1420s, the military successes of [Joan of Arc](/wiki/Joan_of_Arc "Joan of Arc") (d. 1431) led to the victory of the French and the capture of the last English possessions in southern France in 1453.[[289]](#cite_note-Watts317-321) The price was high, as the population of France at the end of the Wars was likely half what it had been at the start of the conflict. Conversely, the Wars positively affected [English national identity](/wiki/English_national_identity "English national identity"), doing much to fuse the various local identities into a national English ideal. The conflict with France also helped create a national culture in England separate from French culture, which had previously been the dominant influence.[[290]](#cite_note-Davies423-322) The dominance of the English [longbow](/wiki/Longbow "Longbow") began during early stages of the Hundred Years' War,[[291]](#cite_note-Nicholle186-323) and cannon appeared on the battlefield at Crécy in 1346.[[245]](#cite_note-Nicolle296-272)

In modern-day Germany, the [Holy Roman Empire](/wiki/Holy_Roman_Empire "Holy Roman Empire") continued to rule, but the elective nature of the imperial crown meant there was no enduring dynasty around which a strong state could form.[[292]](#cite_note-Watts170-324) Further east, the kingdoms of [Poland](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Poland_(1385%E2%80%931569) "Kingdom of Poland (1385–1569)"), [Hungary](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Hungary "Kingdom of Hungary"), and [Bohemia](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Bohemia "Kingdom of Bohemia") grew powerful.[[293]](#cite_note-Watts173-75-325) In Iberia, the Christian kingdoms continued to gain land from the Muslim kingdoms of the peninsula;[[294]](#cite_note-Watts173-326) Portugal concentrated on expanding overseas during the 15th century, while the other kingdoms were riven by difficulties over royal succession and other concerns.[[295]](#cite_note-Watts327-327)[[296]](#cite_note-Watts340-328) After losing the Hundred Years' War, England went on to suffer a long civil war known as the [Wars of the Roses](/wiki/Wars_of_the_Roses "Wars of the Roses"), which lasted into the 1490s[[296]](#cite_note-Watts340-328) and only ended when [Henry Tudor](/wiki/Henry_VII_of_England "Henry VII of England") (r. 1485–1509 as Henry VII) became king and consolidated power with his victory over [Richard III](/wiki/Richard_III_of_England "Richard III of England") (r. 1483–1485) at [Bosworth](/wiki/Battle_of_Bosworth_Field "Battle of Bosworth Field") in 1485.[[297]](#cite_note-Davies426-329) In Scandinavia, [Margaret I of Denmark](/wiki/Margaret_I_of_Denmark "Margaret I of Denmark") (r. in Denmark 1387–1412) consolidated Norway, Denmark, and Sweden in the [Union of Kalmar](/wiki/Union_of_Kalmar "Union of Kalmar"), which continued until 1523. The major power around the Baltic Sea was the Hanseatic League, a commercial confederation of city-states that traded from Western Europe to Russia.[[298]](#cite_note-Davies431-330) Scotland emerged from English domination under [Robert the Bruce](/wiki/Robert_the_Bruce "Robert the Bruce") (r. 1306–1329), who secured papal recognition of his kingship in 1328.[[299]](#cite_note-Davies408-331)

### Collapse of Byzantium

Main articles: [Decline of the Byzantine Empire](/wiki/Decline_of_the_Byzantine_Empire "Decline of the Byzantine Empire"), [Byzantine Empire under the Angelos dynasty](/wiki/Byzantine_Empire_under_the_Angelos_dynasty "Byzantine Empire under the Angelos dynasty"), [Byzantine Empire under the Palaiologos dynasty](/wiki/Byzantine_Empire_under_the_Palaiologos_dynasty "Byzantine Empire under the Palaiologos dynasty"), [Byzantine–Ottoman Wars](/wiki/Byzantine%E2%80%93Ottoman_Wars "Byzantine–Ottoman Wars"), and [Rise of the Ottoman Empire](/wiki/Rise_of_the_Ottoman_Empire "Rise of the Ottoman Empire")

Although the [Palaeologi](/wiki/Palaeologi "Palaeologi") emperors recaptured Constantinople from the Western Europeans in 1261, they could never regain control of much of the former imperial lands. They usually controlled only a small section of the Balkan Peninsula near Constantinople, the city itself, and some coastal lands on the [Black Sea](/wiki/Black_Sea "Black Sea") and around the [Aegean Sea](/wiki/Aegean_Sea "Aegean Sea"). The former Byzantine lands in the Balkans were divided between the new [Kingdom of Serbia](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Serbia_(medieval) "Kingdom of Serbia (medieval)"), the [Second Bulgarian Empire](/wiki/Second_Bulgarian_Empire "Second Bulgarian Empire"), and the city-state of [Venice](/wiki/Republic_of_Venice "Republic of Venice"). A new Turkish tribe threatened the power of the Byzantine emperors, the [Ottomans](/wiki/Ottoman_Empire "Ottoman Empire"), who established themselves in Anatolia in the 13th century and [steadily expanded](/wiki/Byzantine%E2%80%93Ottoman_Wars "Byzantine–Ottoman Wars") throughout the 14th century. The Ottomans expanded into Europe, reducing Bulgaria to a vassal state by 1366 and taking over Serbia after its defeat at the [Battle of Kosovo](/wiki/Battle_of_Kosovo "Battle of Kosovo") in 1389. Western Europeans rallied to the plight of the Christians in the Balkans and declared a new crusade in 1396; a great army was sent to the Balkans, where it was defeated at the [Battle of Nicopolis](/wiki/Battle_of_Nicopolis "Battle of Nicopolis").[[300]](#cite_note-Davies385-332) Constantinople was finally [captured](/wiki/Fall_of_Constantinople "Fall of Constantinople") by the Ottomans in 1453.[[301]](#cite_note-Davies446-333)

### Controversy within the Church

Main articles: [Western Schism](/wiki/Western_Schism "Western Schism"), [Bohemian Reformation](/wiki/Bohemian_Reformation "Bohemian Reformation"), and [Hussites](/wiki/Hussites "Hussites")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/56/07_Gr%C3%A9goire_XI_%28couronn%C3%A9_par_Guy_de_Boulogne%29.jpg/250px-07_Gr%C3%A9goire_XI_%28couronn%C3%A9_par_Guy_de_Boulogne%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:07_Gr%C3%A9goire_XI_(couronn%C3%A9_par_Guy_de_Boulogne).jpg)

[Guy of Boulogne](/wiki/Guy_of_Boulogne "Guy of Boulogne") crowning Pope Gregory XI in a 15th-century miniature from [Froissart's Chronicles](/wiki/Froissart%27s_Chronicles "Froissart's Chronicles")

During the tumultuous 14th century, disputes within the leadership of the Church led to the [Avignon Papacy](/wiki/Avignon_Papacy "Avignon Papacy") of 1309–1376,[[302]](#cite_note-Thomson170-334) also called the "Babylonian Captivity of the Papacy" (a reference to the [Babylonian captivity](/wiki/Babylonian_captivity "Babylonian captivity") of the Jews),[[303]](#cite_note-MAAvignon-335) and then to the [Great Schism](/wiki/Western_Schism "Western Schism"), lasting from 1378 to 1418, when there were two and later three rival popes, each supported by several states.[[304]](#cite_note-MAGreat-336) Ecclesiastical officials convened at the [Council of Constance](/wiki/Council_of_Constance "Council of Constance") in 1414, and in the following year the council deposed one of the rival popes leaving only two claimants. Further depositions followed, and in November 1417, the council elected [Martin V](/wiki/Martin_V "Martin V") (pope 1417–1431) as pope.[[305]](#cite_note-Thomson184-337)

Besides the schism, the Western Church was riven by theological controversies, some of which became heresies. [John Wycliffe](/wiki/John_Wycliffe "John Wycliffe") (d. 1384), an English theologian, was condemned as a heretic in 1415 for teaching that the laity should have access to the text of the Bible as well as for holding views on the [Eucharist](/wiki/Eucharist "Eucharist") that were contrary to Church doctrine.[[306]](#cite_note-Thomson197-338) Wycliffe's teachings influenced two of the major heretical movements of the later Middle Ages: [Lollardy](/wiki/Lollardy "Lollardy") in England and [Hussitism](/wiki/Hussitism "Hussitism") in Bohemia.[[307]](#cite_note-Thomson218-339) The Bohemian movement initiated with the teaching of [Jan Hus](/wiki/Jan_Hus "Jan Hus"), who was burned at the stake in 1415 after being condemned as a heretic by the Council of Constance. The Hussite Church, although the target of a crusade, survived beyond the Middle Ages.[[308]](#cite_note-Thomson213-340) Other heresies were manufactured, such as the accusations against the Knights Templar that resulted in their suppression in 1312, and the division of their great wealth between the French King [Philip IV](/wiki/Philip_IV_of_France "Philip IV of France") (r. 1285–1314) and the Hospitallers.[[309]](#cite_note-Templars-341)

The papacy further refined the practice in the [Mass](/wiki/Mass_(liturgy) "Mass (liturgy)") in the Late Middle Ages, holding that the clergy alone was allowed to partake of the wine in the Eucharist. This further distanced the secular laity from the clergy. The laity continued the practices of pilgrimages, veneration of relics, and belief in the power of the Devil. Mystics such as [Meister Eckhart](/wiki/Meister_Eckhart "Meister Eckhart") (d. 1327) and [Thomas à Kempis](/wiki/Thomas_%C3%A0_Kempis "Thomas à Kempis") (d. 1471) wrote works that taught the laity to focus on their inner spiritual life, which laid the groundwork for the Protestant Reformation. Besides mysticism, belief in witches and witchcraft became widespread. By the late 15th century, the Church had begun to lend credence to populist fears of witchcraft with its condemnation of witches in 1484 and the publication in 1486 of the *[Malleus Maleficarum](/wiki/Malleus_Maleficarum "Malleus Maleficarum")*, the most popular handbook for witch-hunters.[[310]](#cite_note-Davies436-342)

### Scholars, intellectuals, and exploration

See also: [Europeans in Medieval China](/wiki/Europeans_in_Medieval_China "Europeans in Medieval China")

During the Later Middle Ages, theologians such as [John Duns Scotus](/wiki/John_Duns_Scotus "John Duns Scotus") (d. 1308) and [William of Ockham](/wiki/William_of_Ockham "William of Ockham") (d. c. 1348)[[227]](#cite_note-MASchol-252) led a reaction against intellectualist scholasticism, objecting to the application of reason to faith. Their efforts undermined the prevailing [Platonic](/wiki/Platonic_idealism "Platonic idealism") idea of universals. Ockham's insistence that reason operates independently of faith allowed science to be separated from theology and philosophy.[[311]](#cite_note-Davies433-343) Legal studies were marked by the steady advance of Roman law into areas of jurisprudence previously governed by [customary law](/wiki/Customary_law "Customary law"). England was the lone exception to this trend, where the [common law](/wiki/Common_law "Common law") remained pre-eminent. Other countries codified their laws; legal codes were promulgated in Castile, Poland, and [Lithuania](/wiki/Grand_Duchy_of_Lithuania "Grand Duchy of Lithuania").[[312]](#cite_note-Davies438-344)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/51/Studying_astronomy_and_geometry.jpg/250px-Studying_astronomy_and_geometry.jpg)](/wiki/File:Studying_astronomy_and_geometry.jpg)

Clerics studying [astronomy](/wiki/Astronomy "Astronomy") and [geometry](/wiki/Geometry "Geometry"), French, early 15th century

Education remained mostly focused on the training of future clergy. The basic learning of the letters and numbers remained the province of the family or a village priest, but the secondary subjects of the [trivium](/wiki/Trivium "Trivium")—grammar, rhetoric, logic—were studied in cathedral schools or schools provided by cities. Commercial secondary schools spread, and some Italian towns had more than one such enterprise. Universities also spread throughout Europe in the 14th and 15th centuries. Lay literacy rates rose but were still low; one estimate gave a literacy rate of 10 percent of males and 1 percent of females in 1500.[[313]](#cite_note-Singman224-345)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/70/Press_-Bettman.jpg/250px-Press_-Bettman.jpg)](/wiki/File:Press_-Bettman.jpg)

[Gutenberg](/wiki/Johannes_Gutenberg "Johannes Gutenberg") initiated the [spread of printing](/wiki/Global_spread_of_the_printing_press "Global spread of the printing press") from [Mainz](/wiki/Mainz "Mainz"), Germany

The publication of vernacular literature increased, with [Dante](/wiki/Dante "Dante") (d. 1321), [Petrarch](/wiki/Petrarch "Petrarch") (d. 1374) and [Giovanni Boccaccio](/wiki/Giovanni_Boccaccio "Giovanni Boccaccio") (d. 1375) in 14th-century Italy, [Geoffrey Chaucer](/wiki/Geoffrey_Chaucer "Geoffrey Chaucer") (d. 1400) and [William Langland](/wiki/William_Langland "William Langland") (d. c. 1386) in England, and [François Villon](/wiki/Fran%C3%A7ois_Villon "François Villon") (d. 1464) and [Christine de Pizan](/wiki/Christine_de_Pizan "Christine de Pizan") (d. c. 1430) in France. Much literature remained religious, and although a great deal of it continued to be written in Latin, a new demand developed for saints' lives and other devotional tracts in the vernacular languages.[[312]](#cite_note-Davies438-344) This was fed by the growth of the *[Devotio Moderna](/wiki/Devotio_Moderna "Devotio Moderna")* movement, most prominently in the formation of the [Brethren of the Common Life](/wiki/Brethren_of_the_Common_Life "Brethren of the Common Life"), but also in the works of [German mystics](/wiki/German_mystic "German mystic") such as Meister Eckhart and [Johannes Tauler](/wiki/Johannes_Tauler "Johannes Tauler") (d. 1361).[[314]](#cite_note-Keen282-346) Theatre also developed in the guise of [miracle plays](/wiki/Miracle_plays "Miracle plays") put on by the Church.[[312]](#cite_note-Davies438-344) At the end of the period, the development of the [printing press](/wiki/Printing_press "Printing press") by [Johannes Gutenberg](/wiki/Johannes_Gutenberg "Johannes Gutenberg") in about 1450 led to the establishment of publishing houses throughout Europe by 1500.[[315]](#cite_note-Davies445-347)

In the early 15th century, the countries of the [Iberian Peninsula](/wiki/Iberian_Peninsula "Iberian Peninsula") began to sponsor exploration beyond the boundaries of Europe. Prince [Henry the Navigator](/wiki/Henry_the_Navigator "Henry the Navigator") of Portugal (d. 1460) sent expeditions that discovered the [Canary Islands](/wiki/Canary_Islands "Canary Islands"), the [Azores](/wiki/Azores "Azores"), and [Cape Verde](/wiki/Cape_Verde "Cape Verde") during his lifetime. After his death, exploration continued; [Bartolomeu Dias](/wiki/Bartolomeu_Dias "Bartolomeu Dias") (d. 1500) went around the [Cape of Good Hope](/wiki/Cape_of_Good_Hope "Cape of Good Hope") in 1486, and [Vasco da Gama](/wiki/Vasco_da_Gama "Vasco da Gama") (d. 1524) sailed around Africa to India in 1498.[[316]](#cite_note-Davies451-348) The combined Spanish monarchies of Castile and Aragon sponsored the voyage of exploration by [Christopher Columbus](/wiki/Christopher_Columbus "Christopher Columbus") (d. 1506) in 1492 that [discovered the Americas](/wiki/Voyages_of_Christopher_Columbus "Voyages of Christopher Columbus").[[317]](#cite_note-Davies454-349) The English crown under [Henry VII](/wiki/Henry_VII_of_England "Henry VII of England") sponsored the voyage of [John Cabot](/wiki/John_Cabot "John Cabot") (d. 1498) in 1497, which landed on [Cape Breton Island](/wiki/Cape_Breton_Island "Cape Breton Island").[[318]](#cite_note-Davies511-350)

### Technological and military developments

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1b/Crescenzi_calendar.jpg/250px-Crescenzi_calendar.jpg)](/wiki/File:Crescenzi_calendar.jpg)

Agricultural calendar, c. 1470, from a manuscript of [Pietro de Crescenzi](/wiki/Pietro_de_Crescenzi "Pietro de Crescenzi")

One of the major developments in the military sphere during the Late Middle Ages was the increased use of infantry and light cavalry.[[319]](#cite_note-NicolleWestern180-351) The English also employed longbowmen, but other countries were unable to create similar forces with the same success.[[320]](#cite_note-NicolleWestern183-352) Armour continued to advance, spurred by the increasing power of crossbows, and [plate armour](/wiki/Plate_armour "Plate armour") was developed to protect soldiers from crossbows as well as the hand-held guns that were developed.[[321]](#cite_note-NicolleWestern188-353) [Pole arms](/wiki/Pole_arms "Pole arms") reached new prominence with the development of the Flemish and Swiss infantry armed with pikes and other long spears.[[322]](#cite_note-Nicolle185-354)

In agriculture, the increased usage of sheep with long-fibred wool allowed a stronger thread to be spun. In addition, the [spinning wheel](/wiki/Spinning_wheel "Spinning wheel") replaced the traditional [distaff](/wiki/Distaff "Distaff") for spinning wool, tripling production.[[323]](#cite_note-Epstein193-355)[[AG]](#cite_note-357) A less technological refinement that still greatly affected daily life was the use of buttons as closures for garments, which allowed for better fitting without having to lace clothing on the wearer.[[325]](#cite_note-Daily38-358) Windmills were refined with the creation of the [tower mill](/wiki/Tower_mill "Tower mill"), allowing the upper part of the windmill to be spun around to face the direction from which the wind was blowing.[[326]](#cite_note-Epstein200-359) The [blast furnace](/wiki/Blast_furnace "Blast furnace") appeared around 1350 in Sweden, increasing the quantity of iron produced and improving its quality.[[327]](#cite_note-Epstein203-360) The first [patent law](/wiki/Patent_law "Patent law") in 1447 in Venice protected the rights of inventors to their inventions.[[328]](#cite_note-Epstein213-361)

### Late medieval art and architecture

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/02/Les_Tr%C3%A8s_Riches_Heures_du_duc_de_Berry_f%C3%A9vrier.jpg/250px-Les_Tr%C3%A8s_Riches_Heures_du_duc_de_Berry_f%C3%A9vrier.jpg)](/wiki/File:Les_Tr%C3%A8s_Riches_Heures_du_duc_de_Berry_f%C3%A9vrier.jpg)

February scene from the 15th-century illuminated manuscript [Très Riches Heures du Duc de Berry](/wiki/Tr%C3%A8s_Riches_Heures_du_Duc_de_Berry "Très Riches Heures du Duc de Berry")

The Late Middle Ages in Europe correspond to Italy's Trecento and [Early Renaissance](/wiki/Renaissance "Renaissance") cultural periods. Northern Europe and Spain continued to use Gothic styles, which became increasingly elaborate in the 15th century until almost the end. [International Gothic](/wiki/International_Gothic "International Gothic") was a courtly style that reached much of Europe in the decades around 1400, producing masterpieces such as the [Très Riches Heures du Duc de Berry](/wiki/Tr%C3%A8s_Riches_Heures_du_Duc_de_Berry "Très Riches Heures du Duc de Berry").[[329]](#cite_note-Benton253-362) All over Europe secular art continued to increase in quantity and quality. In the 15th century, the mercantile classes of Italy and Flanders became important patrons, commissioning small portraits of themselves in oils as well as a growing range of luxury items such as jewellery, [ivory caskets](/wiki/Casket_with_Scenes_of_Romances_(Walters_71264) "Casket with Scenes of Romances (Walters 71264)"), [cassone](/wiki/Cassone "Cassone") chests, and [maiolica](/wiki/Maiolica "Maiolica") pottery. These objects also included the [Hispano-Moresque ware](/wiki/Hispano-Moresque_ware "Hispano-Moresque ware") produced by mostly [Mudéjar](/wiki/Mud%C3%A9jar "Mudéjar") potters in Spain. Although royalty owned huge plate collections, little survives except for the [Royal Gold Cup](/wiki/Royal_Gold_Cup "Royal Gold Cup").[[330]](#cite_note-363) Italian silk manufacture developed so that Western churches and elites no longer needed to rely on imports from Byzantium or the Islamic world. In France and Flanders, [tapestry](/wiki/Tapestry "Tapestry") weaving of sets like *[The Lady and the Unicorn](/wiki/The_Lady_and_the_Unicorn "The Lady and the Unicorn")* became a major luxury industry.[[331]](#cite_note-Benton257-364)

The large external sculptural schemes of Early Gothic churches gave way to more sculpture inside the building, as tombs became more elaborate and other features such as pulpits were sometimes lavishly carved, as in the [Pulpit by Giovanni Pisano in Sant'Andrea](/wiki/Pulpit_of_Sant%27_Andrea,_Pistoia_(Giovanni_Pisano) "Pulpit of Sant' Andrea, Pistoia (Giovanni Pisano)"). Painted or carved wooden relief [altarpieces](/wiki/Altarpiece "Altarpiece") became common, especially as churches created many [side-chapels](/wiki/Chapel "Chapel"). [Early Netherlandish painting](/wiki/Early_Netherlandish_painting "Early Netherlandish painting") by artists such as [Jan van Eyck](/wiki/Jan_van_Eyck "Jan van Eyck") (d. 1441) and [Rogier van der Weyden](/wiki/Rogier_van_der_Weyden "Rogier van der Weyden") (d. 1464) rivalled that of Italy, as did northern illuminated manuscripts, which in the 15th century began to be collected on a large scale by secular elites, who also commissioned secular books, especially histories. From about 1450, printed books rapidly became popular, though still expensive. There were around 30,000 different editions of [incunabula](/wiki/Incunabula "Incunabula"), or works printed before 1500,[[332]](#cite_note-BL-365) by which time illuminated manuscripts were commissioned only by royalty and a few others. Very small [woodcuts](/wiki/Woodcut "Woodcut"), nearly all religious, were affordable even by peasants in parts of Northern Europe from the middle of the 15th century. More expensive [engravings](/wiki/Engraving "Engraving") supplied a wealthier market with various images.[[333]](#cite_note-Griffiths17-366)

## Modern perceptions

See also: [Dark Ages (historiography)](/wiki/Dark_Ages_(historiography) "Dark Ages (historiography)"), [List of common misconceptions about the Middle Ages](/wiki/List_of_common_misconceptions_about_the_Middle_Ages "List of common misconceptions about the Middle Ages"), [Medieval studies](/wiki/Medieval_studies "Medieval studies"), and [Middle Ages in popular culture](/wiki/Middle_Ages_in_popular_culture "Middle Ages in popular culture")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7c/Gossuin_de_Metz_-_L%27image_du_monde_-_BNF_Fr._574_fo42.jpg/250px-Gossuin_de_Metz_-_L%27image_du_monde_-_BNF_Fr._574_fo42.jpg)](/wiki/File:Gossuin_de_Metz_-_L%27image_du_monde_-_BNF_Fr._574_fo42.jpg)

Medieval illustration of the [spherical Earth](/wiki/Spherical_Earth "Spherical Earth") in a 14th-century copy of *[L'Image du monde](/wiki/Gautier_de_Metz "Gautier de Metz")*

The medieval period is frequently caricatured as a "time of ignorance and superstition" that placed "the word of religious authorities over personal experience and rational activity."[[334]](#cite_note-367) This is a legacy from both the [Renaissance](/wiki/Renaissance "Renaissance") and [Enlightenment](/wiki/Age_of_Enlightenment "Age of Enlightenment") when scholars favourably contrasted their intellectual cultures with those of the medieval period. Renaissance scholars saw the Middle Ages as a period of decline from the high culture and civilisation of the Classical world. Enlightenment scholars saw reason as superior to faith and thus viewed the Middle Ages as a time of ignorance and superstition.[[16]](#cite_note-Davies291-17)

Others argue that reason was held in high regard during the Middle Ages. Science historian [Edward Grant](/wiki/Edward_Grant "Edward Grant") writes, "If revolutionary rational thoughts were expressed [in the 18th century], they were only made possible because of the long medieval tradition that established the use of reason as one of the most important of human activities".[[335]](#cite_note-Grant9-368) Also, contrary to common belief, [David Lindberg](/wiki/David_C._Lindberg "David C. Lindberg") writes, "the late medieval scholar rarely experienced the coercive power of the Church and would have regarded himself as free (particularly in the natural sciences) to follow reason and observation wherever they led".[[336]](#cite_note-QPeters-369)

The caricature of the period is also reflected in some more specific notions. One misconception, first propagated in the 19th century[[337]](#cite_note-flat-370) and still very common, is that all people in the Middle Ages believed that the [Earth was flat](/wiki/Myth_of_the_flat_Earth "Myth of the flat Earth").[[337]](#cite_note-flat-370) This is untrue, as lecturers in medieval universities commonly argued that evidence showed the Earth was a sphere.[[338]](#cite_note-371) Lindberg and [Ronald Numbers](/wiki/Ronald_Numbers "Ronald Numbers"), another scholar of the period, state that there "was scarcely a Christian scholar of the Middle Ages who did not acknowledge [Earth's] sphericity and even know its approximate circumference".[[339]](#cite_note-372) Other misconceptions such as "the Church prohibited autopsies and dissections during the Middle Ages", "the rise of Christianity killed off ancient science", or "the medieval Christian Church suppressed the growth of natural philosophy", are all cited by Numbers as examples of widely popular myths that still pass as historical truth, although they are not supported by historical research.[[340]](#cite_note-Numberslect-373)

29. **[^](#cite_ref-300)** One town, [Lübeck](/wiki/L%C3%BCbeck "Lübeck") in Germany, lost 90 percent of its population to the Black Death.[[271]](#cite_note-Daily189-299)
30. **[^](#cite_ref-309)** As happened with the [Bardi](/wiki/Bardi_family "Bardi family") and [Peruzzi](/wiki/Peruzzi "Peruzzi") firms in the 1340s when King [Edward III of England](/wiki/Edward_III_of_England "Edward III of England") repudiated their loans to him.[[279]](#cite_note-Keen237-308)
31. **[^](#cite_ref-317)** Edward's nickname probably came from his black armour, and was first used by [John Leland](/wiki/John_Leland_(antiquary) "John Leland (antiquary)") in the 1530s or 1540s.[[286]](#cite_note-BP242-316)
32. **[^](#cite_ref-319)** Calais remained in English hands until 1558.[[287]](#cite_note-Davies545-318)
33. **[^](#cite_ref-357)** This wheel was still simple, as it did not yet incorporate a treadle-wheel to twist and pull the fibres. That refinement was not invented until the 15th century.[[324]](#cite_note-Daily36-356)
